Doctor_English: How long has your child been coughing, and does it worsen at night or with activity?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi ti ń sọ̀rọ̀ ikọ́ fún ọjọ́ mẹ́fà. Ó máa ń burú sí i ní àárọ̀ àti nígbà tí ó bá ń sáré.
Clinical_Translation_English: The child has experienced a productive cough for six days. Symptoms are exacerbated nocturnally and upon physical exertion.

Doctor_English: Can you describe the character of your chest pain — is it crushing, stabbing, or radiating to your arm or jaw?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà dájú gan-an, bí ẹni pé wọ́n ń gún mi. Ó ń tan sí apá òsì mi àti ẹ̀gbẹ́ ọrùn mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Patient reports acute, stabbing chest pain with radiation to the left upper extremity and the cervical region.

Doctor_English: Does the chest pain worsen when you breathe in deeply or change position?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, nígbà tí mo bá mí jìnnà, irora náà máa ń burú sí i. Ó máa ń yẹ̀ díẹ̀ nígbà tí mo bá dúró gọ́gọ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Chest pain is exacerbated by deep inspiration and partially relieved by upright positioning, consistent with pleuritic involvement.

Doctor_English: Has the abdominal distension been progressive, and is it associated with any changes in bowel habits or urinary output?
Patient_Yoruba: Ikùn mi ti ń pọ̀ sí i díẹ̀díẹ̀ fún ọjọ́ méjìlá. Ìgbẹ́ mi ti di àìlera, àti pé mo ń ṣe ito díẹ̀ ní báyìí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Progressive abdominal distension over twelve days, accompanied by constipation and oliguria.

Doctor_English: Is the visual disturbance affecting one eye or both, and have you experienced any associated headache or floaters?
Patient_Yoruba: Ojú méjèèjì mi ni wọ́n ń rọ. Mo tún ní orí fífọ̀ ìgbàgbogbo àti àwọn nǹkan kéékèèké tó ń fò nínú ojú mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Bilateral visual blurring accompanied by persistent headache and photopsia (floaters) over an eleven-day period.

Doctor_English: How high has the fever been, and have you noticed any associated rigors, confusion, or difficulty urinating?
Patient_Yoruba: Ibà mi ga púpọ̀, a fi ọwọ́ kan mí, ara mi gbóná gidigidi. Mo ti ń rìn pẹ̀lẹ́ẹ̀, àti pé àwọn ọmọ mi sọ pé mo ń sọ̀rọ̀ àjèjì.
Clinical_Translation_English: High-grade fever with rigors and reported episodes of altered mentation (confusion noted by family members) over eleven days.

Doctor_English: Is the cough dry or productive, and have you noticed any blood-tinged sputum or associated shortness of breath?
Patient_Yoruba: Ikọ́ mi ní ìtọ́ àwọ̀ àárọ̀-àárọ̀ nínú. Mí mi tún kúrú síwájú sí i ní àwọn ọjọ́ mẹ́ta wọ̀nyí
Clinical_Translation_English: Productive cough with mucopurulent sputum, accompanied by progressive dyspnoea over three days.

Doctor_English: Have you had any recent exposure to dust, smoke, or known allergens, and do you have a prior history of asthma or respiratory illness?
Patient_Yoruba: Mi ò ní àìsàn ẹ̀gbẹ̀ rí. Ṣùgbọ́n o mọ̀ pé ọkọ mi ń mu sìgá nínú ilé wa, ìyẹn ló máa ń jẹ́ kí ikọ́ mi burú.
Clinical_Translation_English: No prior history of asthma or atopic disease. Persistent cough likely precipitated by chronic secondary tobacco smoke exposure in the domestic environment.

Doctor_English: Has the child had any rash, joint pains, or episodes of vomiting alongside the fever?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, ọmọ mi ní àárẹ̀ ara àti ìgbagbe oúnjẹ. Ó ti ègbọn lẹ́ẹ̀mejì ní òní.
Clinical_Translation_English: Pyrexia with associated malaise, anorexia, and two episodes of emesis noted on the day of presentation.

Doctor_English: Do you have a known history of diabetes mellitus or hypertension, and are you currently on any medications?
Patient_Yoruba: Àárọ̀ mi ga. Dọ́kítà ti fún mi ní oògùn ìpàná ẹjẹ̀, ṣùgbọ́n mi ò ti mú u fún ọjọ́ pàtàkì bí mẹ́ta.
Clinical_Translation_English: Known hypertensive patient with documented non-compliance with antihypertensive medication for approximately three days. Visual disturbance likely secondary to uncontrolled hypertension.

Doctor_English: Has the child been tested for malaria, and has there been any convulsion or loss of consciousness during the febrile episodes?
Patient_Yoruba: Àgbàdo kò rà á dáadáa rí. Ó ń fọn àti ní ìbà tó ga. Ó yí padà lẹ́ẹ̀kan nígbà tí ìbà náà ga jù.
Clinical_Translation_English: Child has not undergone malaria screening. Reports high-grade pyrexia with chills and a single episode of febrile convulsion at peak fever.

Doctor_English: Is the abdominal swelling localised or generalised, and is it associated with pain, nausea, or delayed menstruation?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora ìdọ̀tí ní apá ìsàlẹ̀ ikùn ni mo ní. Oṣù mi pẹ̀ẹ̀ fún ọjọ́ àádọ́ta. Mo ń bì àti ríru.
Clinical_Translation_English: Lower abdominal pain with localised distension, associated with amenorrhoea for approximately fifty days, nausea, and vomiting. Ectopic pregnancy must be excluded.

Doctor_English: Did the visual disturbance begin suddenly or gradually, and have you experienced any recent headache or eye pain?
Patient_Yoruba: Ojú mi di rórò lójijì ní ọjọ́ àárọ̀ àárọ̀. Mo ní irora orí tó le àti ìmọ̀lẹ̀ tó ń bani lójú.
Clinical_Translation_English: Sudden-onset bilateral visual blurring with associated severe headache and photophobia, commencing two days prior to presentation.

Doctor_English: Has there been any significant weight loss, night sweats, or contact with a person diagnosed with tuberculosis?
Patient_Yoruba: Àárẹ̀ pọ̀ mi ti ń padà kù, mo ń gbọn ní alẹ́, àti pé ìyá àgbà mi ni a ṣẹ̀ṣẹ̀ mú ibà ẹ̀dọ̀fóró jẹ.
Clinical_Translation_English: Notable weight loss, nocturnal diaphoresis, and a history of household contact with a recently diagnosed pulmonary tuberculosis case.

Doctor_English: Have you been experiencing any eye discharge, redness, or increased sensitivity to light?
Patient_Yoruba: Ojú mi pupa àti omi ń jáde nínú rẹ̀ lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀. Àwọ̀ funfun ló ń jáde. Ìmọ̀lẹ̀ máa ń jẹ́ kí ojú mi ṣàn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Bilateral conjunctival hyperaemia with purulent ocular discharge and photophobia over four days, consistent with conjunctivitis or anterior uveitis.

Doctor_English: Is the abdominal distension worsening after meals, and have you noticed any yellowing of your skin or eyes?
Patient_Yoruba: Ikùn mi máa ń wú sí i lẹ́yìn tí mo bá jẹun. Àwọn ọ̀rẹ́ mi sọ pé ojú mi àti awọ ara mi ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí í di pàálu.
Clinical_Translation_English: Post-prandial abdominal distension worsening over nine days with emerging jaundice (scleral icterus and skin yellowing noted by contacts). Hepatobiliary pathology suspected.

Doctor_English: Have you recently returned from travel, and do you have any difficulty swallowing, sore throat, or urinary symptoms alongside the fever?
Patient_Yoruba: Mi ò rin ìrìn-àjò. Ṣùgbọ́n ọfun mi ń dùn, gbígbé oúnjẹ ń jẹ mí nira, àti pé ìtọ mi ń jó mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: No recent travel history. Pyrexia accompanied by pharyngitis, odynophagia, and dysuria, raising differential diagnoses of upper respiratory tract infection with concurrent urinary tract infection.

Doctor_English: Does the chest pain occur at rest or only with exertion, and do you experience any palpitations or shortness of breath with it?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà máa ń wáyé nígbà tí mo bá ń ṣiṣẹ́. Ọkàn mi máa ń lu yára yára, àti pé mí mi kúrú sí i ní àkókò náà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Exertional chest pain associated with palpitations and dyspnoea, suggestive of ischaemic cardiac aetiology. Onset six days prior.

Doctor_English: Has the child been squinting or rubbing their eyes frequently, and has their school performance been affected recently?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, ọmọ mi ń ti ojú rẹ̀ pa kúrú ní kíláàsù lati le rí. Olùkọ́ rẹ̀ sọ pé ó ti di aláìfiyèsí nínú ẹ̀kọ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Child reported to squint to visualise board at school, with associated decline in academic performance over eleven days, suggesting uncorrected refractive error.

Doctor_English: Did the vision loss occur suddenly, and is it affecting your central or peripheral field of vision?
Patient_Yoruba: Ojú mi di òkùnkùn lójijì ní ọ̀sán àná. Apá àárín ojú ni ó kan jù, inú ojú mi ṣoro kíkan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Sudden-onset central visual field loss noted since yesterday afternoon. Central scotoma suspected; urgent assessment for central retinal artery occlusion or macular pathology indicated.

Doctor_English: Can you point to exactly where the pain is — does it stay in one place or spread?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà wà níbí, ní apá ọ̀tún ikùn mi. Kò ń rìn, ó máa ń dúró síbẹ̀ náà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Pain is localised to the right iliac fossa with no radiation, consistent with appendiceal or ovarian pathology.

Doctor_English: Where exactly is the headache — is it at the front, the back, or on one side of your head?
Patient_Yoruba: Orí mi ń dùn lẹ́yìn orí mi, tó sì ń tan wá sí ẹ̀gbẹ́ ọrùn mi. Ko wà niwájú rárá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Occipital headache with radiation to the cervical region. Anterior cranium uninvolved. Tension-type or cervicogenic headache suspected.

Doctor_English: Is the joint pain affecting your knees, hips, or multiple joints at the same time?
Patient_Yoruba: Orúkún méjèèjì mi ni ń dùn, pàápàá jù lọ ní òwúrọ̀. Ìgbọ̀nsẹ̀ mi tún máa ń jẹ mí lẹ́ẹ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Bilateral knee arthralgia with morning predominance and intermittent hip involvement, raising the differential diagnosis of rheumatoid or osteoarthritis.

Doctor_English: Is the back pain in your lower back, upper back, or does it radiate down your leg?
Patient_Yoruba: Ẹ̀hin ìsàlẹ̀ mi ni ó ń dùn. Irora náà máa ń rọ sínú ẹsẹ̀ ọ̀tún mi, títí dé ìpẹ̀sẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Low back pain with right-sided sciatica radiating to the foot, consistent with lumbar disc herniation or nerve root compression.

Doctor_English: Is the skin rash confined to one area or has it spread across your body?
Patient_Yoruba: Àárọ̀ tí àpá mi bẹ̀rẹ̀, lẹ́yìn náà ó tàn sí ọrùn mi àti àyà mi. Ó ń yọ ní gbogbo ibùgbé wọ̀nyí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Rash initially localised to the upper limbs with subsequent centripetal spread to the neck and trunk, raising concern for systemic dermatological or viral aetiology.

Doctor_English: Where do you feel the burning sensation when you pass urine — inside or at the opening?
Patient_Yoruba: Nígbà tí mo bá ń ṣe ito, irora tó dà bí iná ń jó mi ní ẹnu ìtọ mi, kò wà nísàlẹ̀ ikùn rárá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Dysuria localised to the urethral meatus with no associated suprapubic pain, suggesting distal urinary tract involvement or urethritis.

Doctor_English: Is the swelling in your legs affecting the ankles, calves, or the entire leg?
Patient_Yoruba: Ẹsẹ̀ méjèèjì mi ti wú, láti ìsàlẹ̀ ẹsẹ̀ títí dé orúkún. Àárọ̀ ni ó máa ń burú jù.
Clinical_Translation_English: Bilateral pitting oedema from the ankles to the knees, worsening in the morning. Cardiac, renal, or hepatic aetiology must be evaluated.

Doctor_English: Is the sore throat on one side or both sides, and can you feel any lumps in your neck?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọfun mi ń dùn ní apá kan ṣoṣo, ní ọ̀tún. Mo tún lóye pé èso kan wà ní ọrùn mi ní ẹ̀gbẹ́ kan náà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Unilateral right-sided pharyngeal pain with an ipsilateral palpable cervical lymph node, raising concern for peritonsillar abscess or lymphadenopathy.

Doctor_English: Where do you feel the tightness — is it in your chest, throat, or both?
Patient_Yoruba: Àyà mi ni ó ń dín mọ́, bí ẹni pé nǹkan wà tó ń tẹ ọ. Ọfun mi kò kan nínú rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Retrosternal chest tightness without pharyngeal involvement, consistent with cardiac or pulmonary pathology rather than upper airway disease.

Doctor_English: Is the abdominal pain around your navel, or has it moved to the right lower side?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní àárín ikùn mi, nitòsí ibi tí àgọ̀ mi wà. Ní báyìí, ó ti rìn wá sí apá ọ̀tún ìsàlẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Periumbilical pain migrating to the right iliac fossa — a classical presentation of acute appendicitis requiring urgent surgical assessment.

Doctor_English: On a scale of one to ten, how would you rate the severity of your pain right now?
Patient_Yoruba: Bí a bá fi ọgọ́rùn-ún wọ irora, irora mi wà ní ẹjọ. Ó le gan-an, kò jẹ́ kí n sùn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Patient rates pain severity at eight out of ten, describing it as severe and sleep-disruptive, indicative of significant acute pain burden.

Doctor_English: How severe is the dizziness — does it prevent you from standing or walking?
Patient_Yoruba: Orí mi yípo yípo tó jẹ́ kí n ṣubú lẹ́ẹ̀kan. Mo ti dawọ iṣẹ́ dúró nítorí rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe vertiginous episodes resulting in a fall and cessation of occupational activities, suggesting central or peripheral vestibular pathology.

Doctor_English: How intense is your breathing difficulty — can you complete a full sentence without stopping?
Patient_Yoruba: Mí mi kúrú tobẹ̀ẹ́ tí mi ò lè pari ọ̀rọ̀ kan láìdáwọ dúró. Mo tilẹ̀ ń rí ìṣòro láti jókòó.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe dyspnoea precluding completion of full sentences and limiting the patient to sitting position, consistent with acute respiratory compromise.

Doctor_English: How bad is the itching — is it interfering with your sleep or daily activities?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìmọ̀ ara mi le tó jẹ́ kí n jí ní àárọ̀. Mo ti ń gbá ara mi títí dé ibi tí ẹjẹ̀ ń jáde.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe pruritus disrupting sleep and resulting in excoriation with bleeding, indicating a significant dermatological condition requiring urgent evaluation.

Doctor_English: How severe is the nausea — have you been unable to keep any food or water down?
Patient_Yoruba: Ríru mi le, mo ń bì gbogbo ohun tí mo bá jẹ tàbí mu. Ko lọ fún ọjọ́ méjì báyìí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe nausea with persistent vomiting for two days resulting in inability to tolerate oral intake, raising concern for dehydration and electrolyte imbalance.

Doctor_English: How severe is your fatigue — can you still carry out your normal household duties?
Patient_Yoruba: Àárẹ̀ mi le tó jẹ́ kí n máà lè jẹun pa àwọn ọmọ mi. Mo ń wà lórí ibùsùn fún ọjọ́ mẹ́ta.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe fatigue rendering the patient bedridden for three days and unable to perform basic domestic tasks, suggesting systemic illness or haematological pathology.

Doctor_English: How severe is the ear pain — is it affecting your ability to hear on that side?
Patient_Yoruba: Etí mi ń dùn gan-an, ní apá òsì. Gbígbọ́ mi di àìtó ní etí yẹn fún ọjọ́ mẹ́rin.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe left-sided otalgia with ipsilateral hearing impairment over four days, suggestive of acute otitis media or external ear pathology.

Doctor_English: Is the pain severe enough that you needed someone to help you get here today?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, ọkọ mi ni ó gbé mi wá. Mi ò lè rìn nítorí irora ẹsẹ̀ mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Pain severity necessitated assisted ambulation and transport. Patient unable to walk independently due to lower limb pain, indicating significant functional impairment.

Doctor_English: How severe is the abdominal cramp — does it come in waves or is it constant?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà máa ń wáyé ní ìgbà-ìgbà, ó sì le gidigidi nígbà tí ó bá dé. Ó máa ń lọ lẹ́yìn ìṣẹ́jú díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Colicky abdominal pain occurring in intermittent waves of high intensity with spontaneous resolution, consistent with visceral smooth muscle pathology such as ureteric or biliary colic.

Doctor_English: How severe was the convulsion — did the child lose consciousness or stop breathing during it?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi gbọn gan-an, ojú rẹ̀ yípadà. Ó dà bíi pé ó dáwọ mí dúró fún ìṣẹ́jú díẹ̀, ó sì padà sí i.
Clinical_Translation_English: Tonic-clonic convulsive episode with reported transient apnoea and loss of consciousness, requiring urgent neurological assessment and airway management evaluation.

Doctor_English: Is the pain constant throughout the day, or does it come and go at certain times?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà kò ń dúró, ó máa ń wá lọ. Ó máa ń burú ní àárọ̀ àti ní àsìkò oúnjẹ alẹ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Intermittent pain with morning and evening exacerbation, suggesting a pattern consistent with inflammatory joint disease or peptic ulcer disease depending on localisation.

Doctor_English: Does the headache occur every day, or only on certain days of the week?
Patient_Yoruba: Kò ní ọjọ́ tí orí mi ò ń fọ̀. Ó ti jẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ fún ọ̀sẹ̀ méjì báyìí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Daily headache persisting for two weeks without remission, raising concern for chronic daily headache, raised intracranial pressure, or medication-overuse headache.

Doctor_English: Does the chest pain happen at any particular time — after meals, at rest, or only at night?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora àyà mi máa ń wáyé ní àárọ̀ alẹ́ nígbà tí mo bá fẹ́ sùn. Ó kò ń ṣẹlẹ̀ nígbà tí mo bá jẹun.
Clinical_Translation_English: Nocturnal chest pain unrelated to meals, raising concern for gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, variant angina, or musculoskeletal aetiology.

Doctor_English: How long does each episode of palpitation last — seconds, minutes, or longer?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọkàn mi máa ń lu yára yára fún ìṣẹ́jú márùn-ún sí mẹ́wàá. Lẹ́yìn náà ó máa ń padà sí ìwọ̀n àárọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Paroxysmal palpitations lasting five to ten minutes with spontaneous termination, consistent with supraventricular tachycardia or paroxysmal atrial fibrillation.

Doctor_English: Does the shortness of breath occur only at night, or throughout the day as well?
Patient_Yoruba: Mí mi kúrú jù ní àárọ̀ alẹ́, ó máa ń jí mi dìde. Ní ọ̀sán, ó máa ń yẹ̀ díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea with partial diurnal relief, a hallmark presentation of left ventricular failure and pulmonary congestion.

Doctor_English: Does the diarrhoea occur at night as well, or only during the day?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìgbẹ́ gbuuru mi máa ń wáyé ní ọ̀sán àti alẹ́. Ó jí mi dìde lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́ta ní òru.
Clinical_Translation_English: Nocturnal diarrhoea occurring up to three times per night in addition to daytime episodes, suggesting an organic rather than functional aetiology such as inflammatory bowel disease.

Doctor_English: How often do the dizzy spells come — several times a day or occasionally?
Patient_Yoruba: Orí mi máa ń yípo lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́fà sí mẹ́jọ ní ọjọ́ kan. Ó máa ń wáyé lójijì láìsí ìkìlọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Frequent vertiginous episodes occurring six to eight times daily with sudden onset and no prodromal warning, consistent with Ménière's disease or benign paroxysmal positional vertigo.

Doctor_English: Does the fever spike at a particular time of day, or is it persistent throughout?
Patient_Yoruba: Ibà mi máa ń ga jù ní òru, àti ní àárọ̀ ìmọ́lẹ̀. Ní ọ̀sán, ó máa ń rọ díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Biphasic fever pattern with peaks in the early morning and evening with partial diurnal defervescence, a pattern associated with malaria, typhoid fever, and certain viral infections.

Doctor_English: When did the rash first appear, and has it changed in appearance or spread since then?
Patient_Yoruba: Àpá rẹ̀ bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ọjọ́ mẹ́ta sẹ́yìn, ní àwọ̀ pupa. Ní báyìí, ó ti di nǹkan tó ń jẹ àti tó ti tan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Erythematous rash of three days duration with progressive vesiculation and centrifugal spread, raising concern for varicella, herpes zoster, or contact dermatitis.

Doctor_English: Does the urinary frequency happen mostly at night, or equally during the day?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo máa ń jí dìde lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́rin sí márùn-ún ní òru láti ṣe ito. Ní ọ̀sán, mo ń ṣe ito ní ìgbà àárọ̀ péré.
Clinical_Translation_English: Nocturia occurring four to five times per night with relatively normal diurnal frequency, suggesting benign prostatic hyperplasia, diabetes insipidus, or nocturnal polyuria syndrome.

Doctor_English: Did the pain start suddenly or did it develop gradually over several days?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà wáyé lójijì gan-an, bí ẹni pé nǹkan ya nínú ìdí mi. Ko ní ìkìlọ̀ kankan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Sudden-onset severe pain described as a tearing sensation with no prodrome, requiring urgent exclusion of vascular emergencies such as aortic dissection or visceral perforation.

Doctor_English: Were you doing anything specific when the chest pain first started?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ń gbé àpótí wúwo nígbà tí irora náà bẹ̀rẹ̀. Mo ni láti fi sílẹ̀ kí n le dúró mú ìmí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Chest pain precipitated by heavy lifting, with associated dyspnoea requiring cessation of activity. Musculoskeletal or exertional cardiac aetiology must be differentiated.

Doctor_English: Did the weakness in your limbs come on suddenly, like when you woke up, or has it been slowly getting worse?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo jí ní àárọ̀ ọjọ́ kan, apá òsì mi sì kò lè gbe mọ́. Ó wáyé lójijì, kò jẹ́ bẹ̀rẹ̀ díẹ̀díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Acute-onset unilateral upper limb weakness noted on waking, without gradual progression. This presentation is consistent with a cerebrovascular accident and requires immediate neurological evaluation.

Doctor_English: When did you first notice the swelling in your leg, and was there any injury or long journey before it started?
Patient_Yoruba: Ẹsẹ̀ mi bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí wú lẹ́yìn ìrìn-àjò gígùn ní ọkọ̀ ayọ́kẹ́lẹ́ fún wákàtí mẹ́jọ. Mo kò dìde rárá ní ìrìn àjò yẹn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Unilateral leg swelling following prolonged immobility during an eight-hour road journey. Deep vein thrombosis must be urgently excluded given the clinical context.

Doctor_English: Did you notice the yellow colour in your eyes first, or did your skin turn yellow at the same time?
Patient_Yoruba: Ojú mi ni àwọn ọ̀rẹ́ mi sọ pé ó di pàálu kíákíá. Awọ ara mi tún yí padà lẹ́yìn ọjọ́ méjì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Scleral icterus noted first by contacts, with subsequent dermal jaundice appearing two days later, suggesting acute hepatobiliary pathology with progressive hyperbilirubinaemia.

Doctor_English: Did the confusion start after a fall, or was it already present before any injury?
Patient_Yoruba: Ó ṣubú ní ilé, lẹ́yìn náà ni àwọn ẹbí rẹ̀ sọ pé inú rẹ̀ di àásìkí. Ṣùgbọ́n ojúlówó ó ń dàrú ṣáájú ìṣubú náà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Pre-existing confusion confirmed by family prior to the fall event, suggesting the cognitive impairment caused the fall rather than resulting from it. Metabolic or neurological aetiology must be assessed.

Doctor_English: Did the vomiting start before or after the abdominal pain began?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora ikùn mi wáyé kíákíá, ìgbẹ́ èébì sì bẹ̀rẹ̀ lẹ́yìn ìṣẹ́jú mẹ́wàá. Àtẹlẹ irora ni ó wáyé ṣáájú.
Clinical_Translation_English: Abdominal pain preceding the onset of vomiting by approximately ten minutes, a sequence more consistent with a surgical abdomen than with primary gastroenteritis.

Doctor_English: When the rash first appeared, did you have a fever or feel generally unwell at the same time?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, ibà wáyé kíákíá ṣáájú kí àpá náà tó fara hàn. Mo tún ní àárẹ̀ àti irora ara.
Clinical_Translation_English: Prodromal febrile illness with malaise preceding cutaneous eruption, a sequence consistent with viral exanthem, measles, or dengue fever.

Doctor_English: Did the difficulty swallowing start with solids first, or were you unable to swallow liquids from the beginning?
Patient_Yoruba: Ohun tó le ni mi ò lè gbé àárọ̀. Ní báyìí, omi tún ti ń jẹ mí nira. Ó bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ọ̀sẹ̀ mẹ́ta sẹ́yìn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Progressive dysphagia commencing with solids three weeks ago and now including liquids, a pattern highly suggestive of oesophageal malignancy or severe stricture requiring urgent endoscopic evaluation.

Doctor_English: Did the hearing loss in your ear come on suddenly, or have you been losing it gradually for some time?
Patient_Yoruba: Gbígbọ́ etí mi wọ lójijì ní òru, láìsí irora kankan. Mo jí dìde àti pé n ò gbọ́ ohun mọ́ ní etí yẹn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Sudden sensorineural hearing loss with nocturnal onset and no associated otalgia, constituting an otological emergency requiring urgent audiological assessment and corticosteroid therapy.

Doctor_English: How old is the child, and has this type of illness happened before in the family?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi jẹ́ ọmọ ọdún mẹ́ta. Ìyá rẹ̀ náà ní àìsàn bẹ́ẹ̀ nígbà tí ó wà lọ́mọdé.
Clinical_Translation_English: Three-year-old child with a maternal family history of similar illness in childhood, raising the possibility of a hereditary or familial condition warranting genetic screening.

Doctor_English: Are you currently breastfeeding, and have you noticed any changes in your milk supply or breast tenderness?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ń fun ọmọ mi ọmú. Ọmú mi ti di lile àti irora ní apá ọ̀tún fún ọjọ́ mẹ́ta. Wàrà mi tún di díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Lactating patient presenting with right breast induration, tenderness, and reduced milk output over three days, consistent with lactational mastitis requiring antibiotic therapy and continued breastfeeding support.

Doctor_English: Has the child been vaccinated according to the national immunisation schedule?
Patient_Yoruba: A ti fún un àjesara tó yẹ gbogbo. Mo ní ìwé àjesara rẹ̀ tí mo mú wá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Child is reported to be fully immunised according to the national schedule, with vaccination records available for review. This reduces the likelihood of vaccine-preventable aetiologies.

Doctor_English: Do you smoke, drink alcohol, or use any traditional herbal medicines regularly?
Patient_Yoruba: Mi ò mu sìgá, ṣùgbọ́n mo máa ń mu ògógóró lẹ́ẹ̀kan ní ọ̀sẹ̀. Mo tún máa ń mu àgbo oògùn tí agbàdo mi fún mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Patient denies tobacco use but reports weekly alcohol consumption and regular use of traditional herbal preparations. Hepatotoxicity and drug-herb interactions must be considered in the clinical assessment.

Doctor_English: Is there any family history of heart disease, stroke, or sudden death at a young age?
Patient_Yoruba: Bàbá mi kú lójijì ní ọjọ́-orí àádọ́ta. Ẹ̀gbọ́n mi tún ní àìsàn ọkàn. Mo bẹ̀rù pé irora àyà mi ní iṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú ìdílé wa.
Clinical_Translation_English: Paternal sudden cardiac death at age fifty and a sibling with established cardiac disease. Significant familial cardiovascular risk requiring thorough cardiological evaluation including ECG and echocardiography.

Doctor_English: Have you lost your appetite recently, and have you noticed any change in your stool colour?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìfẹ́kúfẹ̀ẹ́ mi ti lọ fún ọjọ́ mẹ́wàá. Ìgbẹ́ mi ti di àwọ̀ funfun-funfun, èyí tí kò jẹ́ àárọ̀ mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Anorexia persisting for ten days with pale acholic stools, strongly suggesting cholestatic liver disease or biliary obstruction requiring urgent hepatobiliary imaging.

Doctor_English: Has the child been eating and drinking normally, and how many wet nappies have you seen today?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi ti dawọ jíjẹ dúró. Ó ti kọ ọmu lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́ta. Ìwọ̀-toto rẹ̀ kéré, àṣọ ìwọ̀ rẹ̀ kò pọ̀ bí ìgbà àárọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Infant with feeding refusal, three episodes of breastfeeding rejection, and reduced wet nappies compared to baseline, indicating significant dehydration and requiring urgent fluid assessment.

Doctor_English: Are you pregnant, or is there any possibility that you could be pregnant?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo lérò pé mo lóyún nítorí oṣù mi ti pẹ̀ẹ̀ fún ọjọ́ ogójì. Ìdánwò oyún tí mo ṣe ní ilé fúnni ní àbájáde rere.
Clinical_Translation_English: Patient reports amenorrhoea of forty days duration with a positive home pregnancy test. Intrauterine pregnancy must be confirmed and ectopic pregnancy excluded by urgent pelvic ultrasonography.

Doctor_English: Have you ever been hospitalised before, and if so, what was it for?
Patient_Yoruba: A gbé mi wọ ilé ìwòsàn lẹ́ẹ̀kan nígbà tí mo bí ọmọ kẹta. Wọ́n sọ pé ẹjẹ̀ mi gbẹ, a ṣe mi àjẹjẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Previous hospitalisation for postpartum haemorrhage requiring blood transfusion during the third delivery. Significant obstetric history with risk of recurrent haemorrhage in future pregnancies.

Doctor_English: Do you work in any environment that might expose you to chemicals, dust, or loud noise?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ń ṣiṣẹ́ nínú ilé iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n ń ṣe àwo. Èéfín ń jáde lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀ níbẹ̀, a kò sì máa ń wọ ìbòjú.
Clinical_Translation_English: Occupational exposure to paint fumes in an unventilated factory setting without personal protective equipment. Chronic solvent toxicity and respiratory disease must be considered in the differential diagnosis.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any changes in your urine — colour, smell, or how often you go?
Patient_Yoruba: Ito mi ti di àwọ̀ pupa-pupa fún ọjọ́ méjì. Ó tún ní òórùn tó gbóná, mi ò sì lè dúró tí ó bá fẹ́ wáyé.
Clinical_Translation_English: Haematuria with malodorous urine and urinary urgency for two days, suggesting urinary tract infection with haemorrhagic cystitis or upper tract involvement requiring urine microscopy and culture.

Doctor_English: Has the patient been able to move all four limbs since the accident?
Patient_Yoruba: Lẹ́yìn ìjàǹbá náà, ẹsẹ̀ rẹ̀ méjì kò lè gbe. Ó ń fẹ̀sẹ̀ mú àti sọ pé kò ní ìmọ̀ ní apá ìsàlẹ̀ ìdí rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Post-traumatic bilateral lower limb paralysis with loss of sensation below the waist, indicating possible spinal cord injury at the thoracolumbar junction requiring immediate immobilisation and imaging.

Doctor_English: Is the child walking normally, or have you noticed any limping or refusal to walk?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi ti kọ ìrìn dúró fún ọjọ́ méjì. Nígbà tí a gbìyànjú láti gbé e dìde, ó ń sọkún tó le.
Clinical_Translation_English: Acute refusal to walk with inconsolable crying on weight-bearing for two days in a child, raising concern for septic arthritis, transient synovitis, or occult fracture requiring urgent orthopaedic assessment.

Doctor_English: Are you able to eat soft foods, or has even swallowing water become difficult?
Patient_Yoruba: Omi tún ti ń jẹ mí nira báyìí. Mo gbìyànjú láti mu omi, ó sì padà wá. Ó bẹ̀rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú ohun tó le, lẹ́yìn náà omi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Complete dysphagia now including liquids, with regurgitation on attempted water intake, indicating severe oesophageal obstruction or achalasia requiring emergency evaluation.

Doctor_English: Has the elderly patient been confused at night but clear-headed during the day?
Patient_Yoruba: Àgbàdo mi ń sọ àwọn ọ̀rọ̀ àjèjì ní alẹ́, ó máa ń jí sálẹ̀ ibùsùn. Ní ọ̀sán, ó mọ àwọn ọmọ rẹ̀ mọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Nocturnal confusion with sundowning behaviour and preservation of daytime orientation, a pattern consistent with delirium superimposed on early dementia or metabolic encephalopathy in the elderly.

Doctor_English: Has the patient lost control of their bladder or bowel at any point during the illness?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, ó ti ṣe ito nínú àṣọ rẹ̀ lẹ́ẹ̀mejì, àti pé ó ṣe ìgbẹ́ kan tí kò mọ̀. Èyí kò jẹ́ iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ rárá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Two episodes of urinary incontinence and one episode of faecal incontinence during the illness, representing a significant change from baseline and raising concern for spinal cord pathology or severe metabolic disturbance.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any lumps, bumps, or unusual growths anywhere on your body recently?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo rí ohun tó dà bíi èso ní ẹ̀gbẹ́ ọrùn mi tó ti ń pọ̀ sí i fún ọjọ́ mẹ́wàá. Kò ń dùn, ṣùgbọ́n ó ti pọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Painless, progressively enlarging cervical lymph node over ten days without associated tenderness, raising concern for lymphoma, metastatic malignancy, or chronic infection such as tuberculosis.

Doctor_English: Is the patient able to recognise family members, and has there been any change in personality?
Patient_Yoruba: Ó mọ àwọn ọmọ rẹ̀ àti ìyàwó rẹ̀. Ṣùgbọ́n ìwà rẹ̀ ti yí padà, ó ti di aláìsùúrù àti ibínú ní ìgbà-ìgbà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Preserved recognition of immediate family with documented personality change characterised by irritability and emotional lability, suggesting frontotemporal involvement in the context of neurodegenerative or vascular dementia.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any trembling in your hands, stiffness in your limbs, or slowing of your movements?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọwọ́ mi máa ń mì nígbà tí mo bá fẹ́ gbe nǹkan. Ara mi tún di jáko, àti pé mo máa ń rìn díẹ̀díẹ̀ ju ìgbà àárọ̀ lọ.
Clinical_Translation_English: Resting tremor, limb rigidity, and bradykinesia reported by the patient, forming the classical triad of Parkinson's disease and warranting urgent neurological referral.

Doctor_English: Has the child's fontanelle appeared bulging or sunken, and have you noticed any neck stiffness?
Patient_Yoruba: Orí ọmọ mi ń wu, a fi ọwọ́ kan rẹ̀. Ó tún ń kọ ìgbà tí a bá gbìyànjú láti tẹ ọrùn rẹ̀ wá siwájú.
Clinical_Translation_English: Bulging anterior fontanelle with neck stiffness in a febrile infant — a paediatric emergency indicating suspected bacterial meningitis requiring immediate lumbar puncture and empirical antibiotic therapy.

Doctor_English: How many pillows do you need to sleep comfortably, and do you wake up gasping for air?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo nílò ìrọ̀lẹ́ mẹ́ta láti sùn dáradára. Ní àárọ̀ alẹ́, mo máa ń jí dìde tó ń fẹ mí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Orthopnoea requiring three pillows and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, classic features of left ventricular failure with pulmonary oedema.

Doctor_English: Have you been coughing up any blood, even just a small streak in your sputum?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, mo rí ẹ̀jẹ̀ díẹ̀ nínú ìtọ́ ikọ́ mi ní ìgbà mẹ́ta. Àwọ̀ pupa tó kéré ni.
Clinical_Translation_English: Haemoptysis with blood-streaked sputum on three occasions, requiring urgent exclusion of pulmonary tuberculosis, bronchiectasis, or malignancy.

Doctor_English: Does your breathing difficulty get worse when you lie flat on your back?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, nígbà tí mo bá dùbúlẹ̀ pẹ̀tẹ́lẹ̀, mí mi ń kúrú gan-an. Mo máa ń gbé orí mi ga.
Clinical_Translation_English: Orthopnoea with positional dyspnoea on supine positioning and compensatory head elevation, consistent with diaphragmatic paralysis or cardiac failure.

Doctor_English: Do you make any wheezing or whistling sound when you breathe, especially at night?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, ìró gbóngbón ń jáde nígbà tí mo bá ń mí. Ó máa ń burú ní alẹ́ àti òwúrọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Nocturnal and early morning wheeze with audible expiratory rhonchi, consistent with reversible airway obstruction as seen in bronchial asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

Doctor_English: Have you been in contact with anyone who has been coughing persistently for more than two weeks?
Patient_Yoruba: Ẹ̀gbọ́n mi ń ikọ́ fún ọ̀sẹ̀ mẹ́ta, a ti sọ fún un láti lọ ilé ìwòsàn ṣùgbọ́n kò tíì lọ.
Clinical_Translation_English: Close household contact with a sibling experiencing a cough of three weeks duration who has not sought medical attention, raising significant concern for pulmonary tuberculosis transmission.

Doctor_English: Is the shortness of breath worse during exercise, or does it happen even at rest?
Patient_Yoruba: Mí mi kúrú nígbà tí mo bá ń rìn jíjìn díẹ̀. Nígbà tí mo bá jókòó, ó máa ń yẹ̀ díẹ̀ ṣùgbọ́n kò lọ pátápátá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Dyspnoea on minimal exertion with incomplete relief at rest, indicating moderate to severe cardiorespiratory compromise warranting urgent functional assessment.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed your lips or fingernails turning blue at any point?
Patient_Yoruba: Àwọn ọmọ mi sọ pé ète mi di dúdú-búlúù nígbà tí mo ń gbẹ̀ ikọ́. Mo sì rí pé ìlà ẹsẹ̀ mi tún yí padà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Reported central and peripheral cyanosis during coughing episodes, indicating critical oxygen desaturation requiring immediate pulse oximetry and respiratory support assessment.

Doctor_English: Do you work in a dusty environment, and have you ever worked in a mine or with asbestos?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ṣiṣẹ́ ilé iṣẹ́ iyẹ̀ fún ọdún mẹ́wàá. Erùpẹ̀ ń pọ̀ níbẹ̀, a kò sì máa ń wọ ohun ìdáàbòbò.
Clinical_Translation_English: Ten years of occupational exposure to flour dust without respiratory protective equipment, raising concern for occupational asthma, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, or baker's lung disease.

Doctor_English: Has the cough been getting progressively worse over months, or did it start suddenly?
Patient_Yoruba: Ikọ́ mi ti ń burú síwájú sí i fún oṣù mẹ́fà. Kò bẹ̀rẹ̀ lójijì, ó jẹ́ díẹ̀díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Insidious onset of progressively worsening cough over six months without acute precipitant, a pattern more consistent with chronic pathology such as malignancy, tuberculosis, or interstitial lung disease than with acute infection.

Doctor_English: Do you feel any pain in your chest when you cough, and does it feel different from your usual discomfort?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, nígbà tí mo bá ń gbẹ̀ ikọ́, irora tuntun ń wáyé ní ẹ̀gbẹ́ àyà mi. Ó yàtọ̀ sí irora tí mo ti mọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Pleuritic chest pain occurring exclusively with coughing and distinct from the patient's baseline discomfort, suggesting pleural involvement possibly secondary to pneumonia, pulmonary embolism, or pleuritis.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any blood in your stool, or has the stool been black and tarry?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìgbẹ́ mi ti di dúdú àti lile bí ètọ̀. Ó ní òórùn tó gbóná. Ó ti jẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ fún ọjọ́ mẹ́ta.
Clinical_Translation_English: Melaena for three days with offensive odour, indicating upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage with digested blood, requiring urgent endoscopy and haemodynamic stabilisation.

Doctor_English: Do you have pain before eating, after eating, or does food actually make the pain better?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora mi máa ń wáyé ṣáájú oúnjẹ, nígbà tí ikùn mi bá ń gbẹ. Jíjẹun máa ń mu irora náà lọ fún ìgbà díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Epigastric pain relieved by food intake and worsening in the fasting state, a pattern characteristic of duodenal ulcer disease requiring H. pylori testing and acid suppression therapy.

Doctor_English: Have you vomited anything that looked like coffee grounds or bright red blood?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, ohun tó dà bíi ilẹ̀ kọfì ni mo ègbọn. Àwọ̀ dudu-pupa ló wà nínú rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Haematemesis with coffee-ground vomitus indicating digested blood from an upper gastrointestinal source, most likely peptic ulcer disease or oesophageal varices in the context of liver disease.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed that your abdomen is making loud gurgling sounds, or has it been completely silent?
Patient_Yoruba: Ikùn mi ti di ìdákẹ́jẹ́ẹ́ lápapọ̀, kò ń sọ ohun mọ́. Ó jẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ láti ìgbà tí irora bẹ̀rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Absent bowel sounds since the onset of abdominal pain, indicating ileus or peritonism, both of which constitute surgical emergencies requiring immediate assessment.

Doctor_English: Do you feel a sensation of food sticking in your chest after you swallow?
Patient_Yoruba: Nígbà tí mo bá gbé oúnjẹ, ó máa ń dúró ní àárín àyà mi bí ẹni pé kò ń lọ sísàlẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Oesophageal food bolus sensation at the mid-sternal level following deglutition, consistent with oesophageal dysmotility, stricture, or malignancy requiring endoscopic evaluation.

Doctor_English: Have you had any episodes of passing bright red blood directly from your back passage?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, ẹjẹ̀ pupa ni mo rí nínú ìgbẹ́ mi. Ó wà lórí ìgbẹ́ náà, kò papọ̀ mọ́ rẹ̀. Ó ti jẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́rin.
Clinical_Translation_English: Fresh rectal bleeding with blood coating the stool rather than mixed within it on four occasions, a pattern more consistent with anorectal pathology such as haemorrhoids or anal fissure, though colorectal malignancy must be excluded.

Doctor_English: Does your stomach pain wake you up from sleep, or is it only present during the day?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora ikùn mi máa ń jí mi dìde ní òru. Ó tún wà ní ọ̀sán ṣùgbọ́n kò le tó irora alẹ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Nocturnal abdominal pain disrupting sleep, a feature more indicative of organic pathology such as peptic ulcer disease or inflammatory bowel disease than functional gastrointestinal disorder.

Doctor_English: Have you been passing any gas normally, or have you been completely unable to pass wind?
Patient_Yoruba: Mi ò ti ní ìgbẹ́ afẹ́fẹ́ rárá fún ọjọ́ méjì. Ikùn mi ń wú sí i lójoojúmọ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Absolute constipation with failure to pass flatus for two days and progressive abdominal distension, indicating complete large bowel obstruction requiring urgent surgical review.

Doctor_English: Has anyone in your family been diagnosed with bowel cancer or stomach cancer?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìyá mi kú nítorí àìsàn ikùn ní ọdún mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn. Dọ́kítà sọ pé ó jẹ́ àrùn jẹjẹrẹ nínú ikùn.
Clinical_Translation_English: First-degree family history of gastrointestinal malignancy with maternal death from stomach cancer a decade prior, significantly elevating the patient's lifetime risk and necessitating early endoscopic surveillance.

Doctor_English: Do you have a burning sensation in your throat or chest after meals, and does it taste acidic?
Patient_Yoruba: Nígbà tí mo bá jẹun, ìró iná máa ń wáyé nínú àyà mi tó sì ń dé ọfun mi. Itọ́ ekan máa ń wá sí ẹnu mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Post-prandial retrosternal burning with acid regurgitation reaching the pharynx, pathognomonic of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, worsened by recumbency and large meals.

Doctor_English: Have you been experiencing any numbness or tingling in your hands or feet?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọwọ́ mi méjèèjì ń hu bí ẹni pé àbẹ̀rẹ̀ ń gún wọn, pàápàá jù lọ ní àárọ̀. Ẹsẹ̀ mi tún máa ń pẹ̀ lẹ́ẹ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Bilateral symmetrical distal paraesthesia in the hands and intermittent lower limb involvement, consistent with a peripheral neuropathy pattern seen in diabetes mellitus, vitamin B12 deficiency, or alcohol-related neuropathy.

Doctor_English: Have you had any episodes of losing consciousness or collapsing without warning?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ṣubú lójijì ní ọjà ní ọjọ́ Ìṣẹ́gun. Kò sí ìkìlọ̀ kankan, mo sì kò mọ ohun tó ṣẹlẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Unheralded syncopal episode in a public setting with no prodrome, requiring differentiation between cardiac syncope, vasovagal syncope, and epileptic seizure as a priority.

Doctor_English: Are you finding it difficult to remember recent events, or are you forgetting things more than usual?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo máa ń gbàgbé àwọn nǹkan tí mo ṣe ní ọjọ́ kéyìí. Mo sì ti gbàgbé orúkọ àwọn ọ̀rẹ́ mi tí mo ti mọ̀ tipẹ́tipẹ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Progressive short-term memory impairment with emerging long-term memory involvement, indicating significant cognitive decline consistent with early to moderate Alzheimer's disease or vascular dementia.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any slurring of your speech, or have people told you they cannot understand you?
Patient_Yoruba: Àwọn ọmọ mi sọ pé ọ̀rọ̀ mi ti di àìgbọ́. Mo máa ń gbìyànjú láti sọ ọ̀rọ̀ ṣùgbọ́n kò jáde dáradára.
Clinical_Translation_English: Reported dysarthria with effortful and unintelligible speech noted by family, raising concern for a cerebrovascular event, motor neurone disease, or cerebellar pathology.

Doctor_English: Have you had any difficulty with your vision, such as seeing double or losing vision in one eye?
Patient_Yoruba: Ojú mi ọ̀tún ń ṣàfihàn àwòrán méjì tí mo bá ń wo nǹkan. Ó bẹ̀rẹ̀ lẹ́yìn orí fífọ̀ tó le.
Clinical_Translation_English: Binocular diplopia following a severe headache, raising urgent concern for a posterior communicating artery aneurysm with third nerve palsy or raised intracranial pressure.

Doctor_English: Do you have any difficulty with balance when walking, or do you tend to fall to one side?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo máa ń yí padà sí apá òsì nígbà tí mo bá ń rìn. Mo ti ṣubú lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́ta ní ọ̀sẹ̀ kan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Left-sided lateropulsion with three falls in one week, indicating ipsilateral cerebellar or vestibular pathology requiring neuroimaging to exclude posterior fossa lesion or cerebrovascular disease.

Doctor_English: Has anyone witnessed you having a fit or convulsion, with shaking of your arms and legs?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìyàwó mi rí mi ń gbọn gbogbo ara, apá àti ẹsẹ̀ mi. Ó sọ pé ó pẹ́ fún ìṣẹ́jú marun. Kò mọ̀ mi ní ìgbà yẹn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately five minutes with post-ictal unresponsiveness, constituting a first presentation of seizure requiring urgent neurological evaluation and electroencephalography.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any drooping of your face, or difficulty closing one eye completely?
Patient_Yoruba: Ẹ̀gbẹ́ ọ̀tún ojú mi ti rọ̀, mi ò lè tì ojú mi yẹn dé. Àwọn ọ̀rẹ́ mi sọ pé ẹnu mi ti yí padà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Right-sided facial nerve palsy with lagophthalmos and facial asymmetry, consistent with peripheral facial nerve palsy such as Bell's palsy or secondary to parotid pathology, stroke, or Ramsay Hunt syndrome.

Doctor_English: Do you experience any visual disturbances, flashing lights, or blind spots before your headaches begin?
Patient_Yoruba: Ṣáájú kí orí mi tó dùn, mo máa ń rí ìmọ̀lẹ̀ tó ń yọ̀ àti ibi tó ṣòòfà nínú ojú mi fún ìṣẹ́jú mẹ́wàá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Visual aura characterised by scintillating scotoma lasting approximately ten minutes preceding headache onset, fulfilling the diagnostic criteria for migraine with aura.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any difficulty with fine tasks like buttoning your shirt or writing?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ti dáwọ kikọ dúró nítorí ọwọ́ mi ń mì nígbà tí mo bá gbìyànjú. Dídì bọ́ọ̀tù aṣọ mi tún ti ń ṣòro fún mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Fine motor impairment affecting writing and fastening buttons, combined with a resting tremor, strongly suggestive of early Parkinson's disease or essential tremor requiring neurological assessment.

Doctor_English: Do you feel your heart racing or skipping beats, and does it start and stop suddenly?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọkàn mi máa ń lu yára yára lójijì, lẹ́yìn náà ó máa ń dáwọ lójijì pẹ̀lú. Ó ń jẹ mí lójú nígbà tí ó bá ń ṣẹlẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Paroxysmal palpitations with sudden onset and termination associated with presyncope, consistent with a re-entrant supraventricular tachycardia or paroxysmal atrial fibrillation requiring Holter monitoring.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any swelling in your ankles at the end of the day that improves overnight?
Patient_Yoruba: Ẹsẹ̀ mi méjèèjì máa ń wú ní òrùlé ọjọ́. Àárọ̀, nígbà tí mo bá jí dìde, ó máa ń ti rọ díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Bilateral dependent pitting oedema with diurnal variation — worsening in the evening and improving after overnight recumbency — a hallmark of right-sided or biventricular cardiac failure.

Doctor_English: Have you ever been told your blood pressure is very high, and are you taking any medicines for it?
Patient_Yoruba: Dọ́kítà sọ pé ẹjẹ̀ mi ga ní ọdún méjì sẹ́yìn. Wọ́n fún mi ní oògùn ṣùgbọ́n mo ti dáwọ̀ mu nítorí mi ò nímọ̀lára rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Known hypertension for two years with self-discontinued antihypertensive medication due to perceived absence of symptoms, representing a significant risk for hypertensive emergency, stroke, and target organ damage.

Doctor_English: Do you get pain in your calves when you walk that goes away when you rest?
Patient_Yoruba: Ẹsẹ̀ mi ń dùn nígbà tí mo bá ń rìn jíjìn. Nígbà tí mo bá dúró síta, irora náà máa ń lọ lẹ́yìn ìṣẹ́jú díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Intermittent claudication with reproducible calf pain on exertion relieved by rest, indicating peripheral arterial disease secondary to atherosclerosis and warranting ankle-brachial index measurement.

Doctor_English: Has your face, lips, or tongue ever swollen suddenly without any clear cause?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, ète mi àti ahọ́n mi wú lójijì ní ọjọ́ Ìṣẹ́gun, láìsí ohun tí mo jẹ tuntun. Mo lọ sí ilé ìwòsàn lẹ́ẹ̀kan tẹ́lẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Recurrent angioedema of the lips and tongue with no identifiable trigger, suggesting hereditary angioedema, ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema, or idiopathic allergic aetiology requiring immunological assessment.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any coldness, pallor, or discolouration in one of your legs compared to the other?
Patient_Yoruba: Ẹsẹ̀ ọ̀tún mi ti di tutù àti àwọ̀ rẹ̀ ti yí padà sí funfun. Ó bẹ̀rẹ̀ lójijì ní òru àná.
Clinical_Translation_English: Acute limb ischaemia with sudden onset pallor and coldness of the right lower limb, representing a vascular emergency requiring immediate surgical assessment for arterial occlusion or embolism.

Doctor_English: Do you get chest pain or tightness specifically when you are under emotional stress?
Patient_Yoruba: Nígbà tí inú mi bá dùn púpọ̀ tàbí tí inú mi bá ń bí mi, irora àyà máa ń wáyé. Kò ń ṣẹlẹ̀ nígbà tí mo bá sinmi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Emotionally triggered chest pain occurring exclusively during psychological stress without physical exertion, raising the differential diagnosis of vasospastic angina, Takotsubo cardiomyopathy, or somatisation disorder.

Doctor_English: Have you ever had a heart attack or been told you have blocked arteries?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọdún mẹ́ta sẹ́yìn, mo wọ ilé ìwòsàn nítorí irora àyà tó le. Dọ́kítà sọ pé àlọ̀ ọkàn mi ti dínà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Previous myocardial infarction three years ago with documented coronary artery disease, significantly elevating the risk of recurrent ischaemic events and mandating review of secondary prevention therapy.

Doctor_English: Does your chest pain radiate to your jaw, left arm, or between your shoulder blades?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà ń tan sí apá òsì mi àti ẹ̀gbẹ́ ẹrẹ̀kẹ́ mi. Ó máa ń wáyé nígbà tí mo bá ń gùn àtẹ̀gùn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Ischaemic chest pain radiating to the left upper limb and mandible precipitated by stair climbing, fulfilling the criteria for stable angina pectoris and requiring urgent cardiac evaluation.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any bluish discolouration around your lips or at the tips of your fingers?
Patient_Yoruba: Ète mi àti orí ìlà ọwọ́ mi ti di bíi àwọ̀ búlúù díẹ̀. Ọmọ mi ni ó kọ́kọ́ ríi tó sì mú mi wá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Central and peripheral cyanosis noted by the patient's child, indicating significant arterial oxygen desaturation requiring immediate pulse oximetry, arterial blood gas analysis, and supplemental oxygen therapy.

Doctor_English: How old is the child, and is this the first time they have had this kind of illness?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi jẹ́ ọmọ ọdún kan àbọ̀. Ìgbà àárọ̀ ni ó ń ṣàìsàn bẹ́ẹ̀, ó sì máa ń dára lẹ́yìn oògùn kí ó tó padà.
Clinical_Translation_English: A nineteen-month-old child with a recurrent pattern of similar febrile illness responding to medication before relapsing, raising concern for a chronic underlying condition or recurrent infection such as malaria or urinary tract infection.

Doctor_English: Is the child still feeding well, or have they lost interest in food and the breast?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi ti kọ jíjẹun pátápátá. Ó kọ ọmu àti omi àgbádó. Ó ti jẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ fún ọjọ́ méjì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Complete feeding refusal including breast milk and complementary feeds for two days in a young child, indicating significant illness severity and risk of dehydration requiring urgent clinical assessment.

Doctor_English: Has the child's cry changed — is it weaker, higher-pitched, or does the child seem too quiet?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi ti dẹ́kun sọkún. Kò ń kígbe mọ́ bí ìgbà àárọ̀, ó dà bí ẹni tó ń sun títí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Absent cry with excessive somnolence in an infant, constituting danger signs of severe illness — potentially sepsis, meningitis, or severe anaemia — requiring immediate triage and resuscitation assessment.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any neck stiffness, arching of the back, or unusual eye movements in the child?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi ń tẹ ẹ̀yìn rẹ̀ sẹ́yìn kí ó sì ń kígbe. Ọrùn rẹ̀ dì, kò lè tẹ orí rẹ̀ wá siwájú.
Clinical_Translation_English: Opisthotonus with neck rigidity and inconsolable cry in a febrile child — a classical presentation of bacterial meningitis or tetanus requiring immediate lumbar puncture, blood cultures, and empirical antibiotic therapy.

Doctor_English: Has the child developed any new teeth recently, and could the fever be related to teething?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi ń hù eyín tuntun méjì. Àwọn àgbàlagbà sọ pé ibà rẹ̀ wa nítorí eyín. Ṣùgbọ́n ibà náà ga jù.
Clinical_Translation_English: High-grade fever in a teething infant — while teething may cause mild pyrexia, a temperature exceeding 38.5°C should not be attributed to dentition without excluding infectious aetiology such as otitis media or urinary tract infection.

Doctor_English: Is the child making enough wet nappies, and has their skin lost its normal elasticity?
Patient_Yoruba: Àṣọ ìwọ̀ rẹ̀ kéré gan-an, kò tó ìgbà mẹ́ta lónìí. Awọ ara rẹ̀ tún ti ń gbẹ, nígbà tí a bá gbe rẹ̀, kò padà kíákíá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Reduced wet nappies to fewer than three in a day with loss of skin turgor on examination, indicating clinical dehydration likely exceeding five percent and requiring oral or intravenous rehydration therapy.

Doctor_English: Has the child been scratching their bottom or complaining of itching around the anal area?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi máa ń ní ìyọnu ní ìdí rẹ̀, pàápàá jù lọ ní òru. Ó máa ń gbá ara rẹ̀ ní ibẹ̀ títí dé ìgbà tí ó bá sùn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Nocturnal perianal pruritus in a child causing sleep disruption, the classic presentation of enterobiasis (threadworm infection) requiring anthelmintic treatment and household hygiene measures.

Doctor_English: Has the child been limping or refusing to put weight on one leg after playing?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi pada sí ilé tí kò lè fi ẹsẹ̀ ọ̀tún rẹ̀ tẹ ilẹ̀ lẹ́yìn tí ó ṣeré. Kò ní ìṣubú, ṣùgbọ́n ó ń sọkún nígbà tí a bá fọwọ́ kan ẹsẹ̀ rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Post-exertional non-traumatic non-weight-bearing of the right lower limb with localised tenderness on palpation in a child, requiring urgent exclusion of septic arthritis, transient synovitis, or occult stress fracture.

Doctor_English: Has the child's abdomen appeared bloated, and are you able to see the outline of their intestines through the skin?
Patient_Yoruba: Ikùn ọmọ mi ti wú gan-an, a sì lè rí ìlà ìfun rẹ̀ nípasẹ̀ awọ ara rẹ̀. Ó ń sọkún nígbà tí a bá tẹ ikùn rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Grossly distended abdomen with visible intestinal loops through the abdominal wall and tenderness on palpation in a child, raising urgent concern for intestinal obstruction, malrotation with volvulus, or Hirschsprung's disease.

Doctor_English: Has the child been passing pale, fatty-looking stools that are difficult to flush?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìgbẹ́ ọmọ mi ti di àwọ̀ funfun, tó sì ń fò lórí omi nínú ìgbọ̀nsẹ̀. Ó ní òórùn tó gbóná, ó sì le láti wẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Steatorrhoea characterised by pale, floating, malodorous stools indicating fat malabsorption, raising concern for coeliac disease, cystic fibrosis, or exocrine pancreatic insufficiency in a child.

Doctor_English: How many weeks pregnant are you, and have you been attending your antenatal clinic regularly?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo loyún oṣù márùn-ún. Mo ti lọ sí ìbẹ̀wò oyún lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́ta nìkan nítorí owó àti ọkọ àgbàdo tó jìnnà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Primigravida at twenty weeks gestation with only three antenatal visits due to financial constraints and distance to clinic, indicating poor antenatal coverage and elevated risk for undetected obstetric complications.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any unusual vaginal discharge — is it coloured, smelly, or causing itching?
Patient_Yoruba: Omi tó ń jáde nínú ìbò mi ti yí padà sí àwọ̀ pàálu àti ìrísí líle. Ó ń ṣe mí ìyọnu àti ín-ín.
Clinical_Translation_English: Abnormal vaginal discharge with purulent yellow colour and associated pruritus, consistent with bacterial vaginosis, candidiasis, or a sexually transmitted infection requiring microscopy, culture, and sensitivity testing.

Doctor_English: Have you had any bleeding during this pregnancy, even just light spotting?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo rí ẹjẹ̀ díẹ̀ nínú aṣọ ìbọ mi ní ọjọ́ méjì sẹ́yìn. Kò pọ̀, ṣùgbọ́n ó dúró fún wákàtí díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Antepartum haemorrhage with light spotting lasting several hours two days prior to presentation, requiring urgent obstetric assessment to exclude placenta praevia, placental abruption, or threatened miscarriage.

Doctor_English: Have you been experiencing any pain or burning during sexual intercourse recently?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, ìbálòpọ̀ ti di ìrora fún mi fún ọ̀sẹ̀ mẹ́ta. Irora ń wáyé nínú àti lẹ́yìn ìbálòpọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Dyspareunia of three weeks duration with both superficial and deep components, raising concern for vaginismus, pelvic inflammatory disease, endometriosis, or ovarian pathology.

Doctor_English: Have your periods always been irregular, or is this change something new for you?
Patient_Yoruba: Oṣù mi ti máa ń pẹ̀ẹ̀ àti wáyé ni ìgbà-ìgbà láti ìgbà tí mo wà ní ilé ẹ̀kọ́. Ó ti jẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ rí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Longstanding menstrual irregularity since adolescence rather than an acute change, suggesting polycystic ovarian syndrome, thyroid dysfunction, or a constitutional pattern requiring hormonal evaluation.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any lumps in your breast, and do they change in size around your period?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo rí èso kan nínú ọmú ọ̀tún mi nígbà tí mo ń wẹ̀. Ó máa ń pọ̀ díẹ̀ ṣáájú oṣù mi àti rọ lẹ́yìn rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: A cyclical breast lump in the right breast with perimenstrual size variation, most consistent with a fibroadenoma or fibrocystic change, though malignancy must be excluded by clinical examination and imaging regardless of cyclical behaviour.

Doctor_English: Have you been experiencing any hot flushes, night sweats, or mood changes recently?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo máa ń ní ìgbóná lójijì tó ń tan gbogbo ara, pàápàá ní alẹ́. Ìwà mi tún ti yí padà, mo ti di aláìsùúrù.
Clinical_Translation_English: Vasomotor symptoms with nocturnal diaphoresis and emotional lability in a woman of appropriate age, consistent with perimenopausal transition requiring hormonal and psychological assessment.

Doctor_English: How long have you been trying to conceive, and have you had any investigations done?
Patient_Yoruba: A ti ń gbìyànjú láti bí ọmọ fún ọdún méjì láìsí àṣeyọrí. A ti ṣe ìdánwò díẹ̀ ṣùgbọ́n a kò ti gba àbájáde.
Clinical_Translation_English: Primary infertility for two years with incomplete investigation workup, warranting a systematic evaluation including ovarian reserve testing, semen analysis, tubal patency assessment, and thyroid function.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any increased hair growth on your face or chest, or any acne since your periods became irregular?
Patient_Yoruba: Irun ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí hù lórí ojú mi àti àyà mi. Mo tún ní ẹ̀gbọ̀n ní ojú mi bí ìgbà tí mo wà ní ilé ẹ̀kọ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Hirsutism involving the face and chest with acne vulgaris in the context of menstrual irregularity, forming a triad highly suggestive of polycystic ovarian syndrome with hyperandrogenism.

Doctor_English: Have you had any previous miscarriages, and were you given any explanation for why they occurred?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ti sọ oyún nù lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́ta, ọ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan ní ìsẹ̀yìn kẹ́ta. Dọ́kítà kò sọ ohun tó fa á.
Clinical_Translation_English: Three consecutive first-trimester pregnancy losses without a documented aetiology, fulfilling the diagnostic criteria for recurrent miscarriage and necessitating investigation for antiphospholipid syndrome, chromosomal abnormalities, and uterine structural anomalies.

Doctor_English: Is the joint stiffness worse in the morning and does it improve as the day goes on?
Patient_Yoruba: Ara mi di jáko gbogbo ní òwúrọ̀, pàápàá jù lọ ní àwọn ìpín. Ó máa ń yẹ̀ lẹ́yìn wákàtí méjì sí mẹ́ta.
Clinical_Translation_English: Morning stiffness lasting two to three hours with progressive diurnal improvement, a hallmark feature of inflammatory arthritis such as rheumatoid arthritis, distinguishing it from the brief stiffness of osteoarthritis.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any redness, warmth, or swelling over the painful joint?
Patient_Yoruba: Orúkún mi ọ̀tún pupa, ó sì gbóná nígbà tí a fi ọwọ́ kan rẹ̀. Ó wú sí i ní ọjọ́ méjì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Acute monoarthritis of the right knee with erythema, warmth, and progressive swelling over two days, raising concern for septic arthritis — an orthopaedic emergency — or crystal arthropathy such as gout or pseudogout.

Doctor_English: Have you had any recent injury, fall, or unusual physical activity before the pain started?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo gùn igi láti hái ọ̀pọ̀tọ́ ní ọjọ́ Ìṣẹ́gun, lẹ́yìn náà irora ẹ̀hin mi bẹ̀rẹ̀. Ko sí ìṣubú ṣùgbọ́n ó le.
Clinical_Translation_English: Acute onset low back pain following unaccustomed climbing activity without direct trauma, suggesting acute musculoligamentous strain, though vertebral compression fracture must be excluded particularly if osteoporosis risk is present.

Doctor_English: Do you have any difficulty raising your arm above your head, such as when combing your hair?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ti dáwọ ẹwà irun fún ọwọ́ mi dúró nítorí mi ò lè gbé apá mi gaju. Irora ń wáyé nígbà tí mo bá gbìyànjú.
Clinical_Translation_English: Painful restriction of shoulder abduction and flexion preventing overhead activity, consistent with rotator cuff pathology, adhesive capsulitis, or subacromial impingement syndrome.

Doctor_English: Has the knee been giving way or locking in a bent position, preventing you from straightening it?
Patient_Yoruba: Orúkún mi ti mú mi ṣubú lẹ́ẹ̀mejì nítorí ó dà bí ẹni pé ko ní agbára. Lẹ́ẹ̀kan náà, ó dì, mi ò lè tọ ẹsẹ̀ mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Mechanical knee symptoms comprising giving way episodes and a single locking event, strongly suggesting internal derangement of the knee with possible meniscal tear or anterior cruciate ligament rupture requiring MRI evaluation.

Doctor_English: Do your joints crack or grind when you move them, and is this associated with pain?
Patient_Yoruba: Àwọn ìpín mi máa ń ṣe ìró pẹ́pẹ́ nígbà tí mo bá gbe wọn. Irora ń tẹ̀lé ìró náà ní àwọn orúkún mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Crepitus in the knee joints associated with pain on movement, consistent with articular cartilage degeneration as seen in osteoarthritis, particularly in the context of age and mechanical loading history.

Doctor_English: Have you ever been diagnosed with gout, and have you been eating a lot of red meat or alcohol recently?
Patient_Yoruba: Dọ́kítà sọ pé mo ní ẹ̀jẹ̀ acid uric tó pọ̀ ní ọdún kan sẹ́yìn. Mo ti ń jẹ ẹran pọ̀ àti mimu ọtí ní ọ̀sẹ̀ tí ó kọjá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Known hyperuricaemia with recent dietary indiscretion including excess red meat and alcohol consumption, the classical precipitants of an acute gout flare, consistent with the current presentation of joint inflammation.

Doctor_English: Is the back pain associated with any shooting pain down one or both legs?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora ẹ̀hin mi ń rọ sínú ẹsẹ̀ méjèèjì, tó ń lọ títí dé ìpẹ̀sẹ̀. Apá òsì ni ó le jù.
Clinical_Translation_English: Bilateral sciatica with left-sided predominance radiating to the feet, suggesting central lumbar disc prolapse with potential bilateral nerve root compression, warranting urgent MRI and neurological assessment.

Doctor_English: Have your muscles been feeling weak lately, and do you have difficulty getting up from a chair without using your arms?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ti ń rí ìṣòro láti dìde nínú àga láìfi ọwọ́ gbé ara mi. Ìgbà míìràn, mo kàn ń jókòó tí kò lè dìde.
Clinical_Translation_English: Proximal lower limb muscle weakness evidenced by difficulty rising from a seated position without arm assistance, a pattern consistent with inflammatory myopathy such as polymyositis, or metabolic myopathy.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any skin changes over the painful joint, such as a rash or scaly patches?
Patient_Yoruba: Awọ ara ní àyíká orúkún mi ń tán, ó sì ní àwọn ẹ̀gbọ̀n tó bo àwọ̀ funfun. Irora ìpín bẹ̀rẹ̀ lẹ́yìn àpá náà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Psoriatic plaques overlying a painful joint with arthritis following the skin manifestation, consistent with psoriatic arthritis — a seronegative spondyloarthropathy requiring dermatological and rheumatological co-management.

Doctor_English: Have you been feeling excessively thirsty and passing large amounts of urine more than usual?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo máa ń fẹ omi ní ìgbà-ìgbà, mi ò sì lè yọ ìfẹ́ omi náà. Mo ń ṣe ito lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀, pàápàá ní alẹ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Polydipsia and polyuria with prominent nocturia, forming the classical triad of uncontrolled diabetes mellitus or diabetes insipidus, requiring urgent fasting blood glucose and urinalysis.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any unexplained weight gain, especially around your abdomen, along with stretch marks?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ti pọ̀ sí i ní ìwọ̀ ara ní oṣù mẹ́fà pẹ̀lú ọgbẹ́ aṣọ ní ikùn àti ìdí. Ojú mi tún ti yípo.
Clinical_Translation_English: Central obesity with abdominal striae and facial rounding over six months, raising concern for Cushing's syndrome secondary to glucocorticoid excess, whether exogenous or endogenous.

Doctor_English: Have you been feeling unusually cold all the time, and has your hair or skin changed in texture?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo máa ń ní òtútù nígbà tí àwọn ẹlòmíràn ń ní ìgbóná. Irun mi ti di gbígbẹ àti awọ ara mi ti di líle, bí ṣẹ̀ẹ̀dì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Cold intolerance, dry skin, and coarse hair texture in a patient presenting with fatigue and weight gain, constituting classic features of primary hypothyroidism requiring urgent thyroid function testing.

Doctor_English: Have you been feeling excessively hot, sweating more than usual, and losing weight despite eating well?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ń gbóná lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀ àti ń gbó nígbà tí àwọn ẹlòmíràn kò ń gbó. Mo ti tinrín síwájú sí i bí mo tilẹ̀ jẹ dáradára.
Clinical_Translation_English: Heat intolerance, excessive diaphoresis, and unintentional weight loss despite preserved appetite, forming the classic triad of hyperthyroidism, potentially secondary to Graves' disease or toxic multinodular goitre.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed your neck appearing swollen or a lump at the base of your throat?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọrùn mi ti wú ní ẹ̀gbẹ́ iwájú. Mo máa ń rí i nígbà tí mo bá wò inú dígí. Ó ti ń pọ̀ díẹ̀díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Anterior neck swelling with progressive enlargement visible on self-examination, consistent with thyroid gland pathology — either diffuse goitre, multinodular goitre, or a thyroid nodule requiring ultrasound and thyroid function assessment.

Doctor_English: Have you been getting recurrent infections, or do wounds take much longer to heal than they used to?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọgbẹ́ tí mo gba lẹ́yìn ìgbẹ́ abẹ kéékèéké ko tíì sàn fún ọ̀sẹ̀ mẹ́ta. Mo tún ti ni àkóràn ito lẹ́ẹ̀mejì ní oṣù kan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Impaired wound healing post-minor incision and two urinary tract infections within one month, constituting strong clinical evidence of undiagnosed or poorly controlled diabetes mellitus requiring immediate glycaemic assessment.

Doctor_English: Have you ever been told your blood sugar is high, and do you test your blood at home?
Patient_Yoruba: Dọ́kítà sọ pé sùgà ẹjẹ̀ mi ga ní ọdún kan sẹ́yìn, ṣùgbọ́n mi ò tẹ̀lé ìmọ̀ rẹ̀. Mi ò ń ṣe ìdánwò ẹjẹ̀ mi ní ilé.
Clinical_Translation_English: Known hyperglycaemia identified one year ago with documented non-adherence to dietary advice and no home blood glucose monitoring, indicating poorly managed pre-diabetes or early type two diabetes requiring glycated haemoglobin measurement.

Doctor_English: Have you been experiencing episodes of sweating, shaking, and confusion that improve after eating something sweet?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo máa ń gbọn àti ríru nígbà tí mo bá pẹ̀ láìjẹun. Nígbà tí mo bá jẹ sùgà, gbogbo rẹ̀ máa ń dára sí i lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Symptomatic hypoglycaemia with diaphoresis, tremor, and impaired cognition relieved promptly by carbohydrate intake, consistent with reactive hypoglycaemia or insulinoma, requiring fasting glucose testing and endocrinological evaluation.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any darkening of the skin in the folds of your body, such as your neck or armpits?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọrùn mi àti ìdẹ́ apá mi ti di dúdú síwájú sí i. Mo fẹ́ mọ̀ bóyá ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìsàn tàbí ìwẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Acanthosis nigricans affecting the neck and axillary folds, a cutaneous marker of insulin resistance commonly associated with obesity-related type two diabetes mellitus or, less commonly, internal malignancy.

Doctor_English: Have you been feeling extremely tired, muscle weakness, and salt craving, with darker skin than usual?
Patient_Yoruba: Àárẹ̀ mi le gan-an, iṣan ara mi àì ní agbára, àti pé mo fẹ́ oúnjẹ iyọ̀ lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀. Awọ ara mi tún ti di dúdú sí i.
Clinical_Translation_English: The triad of severe fatigue, proximal myopathy, salt craving, and hyperpigmentation raises strong concern for primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease), a potentially life-threatening condition requiring urgent cortisol measurement and endocrine review.

Doctor_English: How long have you had this rash, and did it start in one place before spreading?
Patient_Yoruba: Àpá náà bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ọwọ́ mi ọ̀tún ní ọjọ́ márùn-ún sẹ́yìn, lẹ́yìn náà ó tàn sí àyà mi àti ẹ̀gbẹ́ ọrùn mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: A unilateral upper limb rash of five days duration with centripetal spread to the trunk and neck, consistent with contact dermatitis, herpes zoster, or a viral exanthem requiring dermatological assessment.

Doctor_English: Does the rash itch, burn, or cause no sensation at all?
Patient_Yoruba: Àpá náà kò ń jẹ mí ín-ín rárá, kò ń jó, kò sì ń ṣe mí ìrora. Ó ń dún nìkan nígbà tí a bá tẹ̀ ẹ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: An anaesthetic rash with tenderness only on direct pressure and no spontaneous pruritus or burning, raising significant concern for leprosy given the hypoaesthesia pattern.

Doctor_English: Have you changed any soaps, creams, or clothing recently around the time the rash appeared?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí lo ọṣẹ tuntun ọjọ́ méjì ṣáájú kí àpá náà tó fara hàn. Mi ò ti lo rẹ̀ rí ṣáájú.
Clinical_Translation_English: Temporal association between introduction of a new soap product and onset of cutaneous eruption, strongly suggesting allergic contact dermatitis requiring patch testing and allergen avoidance.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any blistering, weeping, or crusting of the skin on the affected area?
Patient_Yoruba: Àpá náà ti ṣẹ̀dá àwọn ohun tó dà bíi omi nínú, lẹ́yìn náà wọ́n ya, ó sì ti di ìdìmú ní báyìí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Vesicular eruption progressing to rupture and crusting, consistent with herpes simplex, varicella zoster, or impetigo, requiring viral swab and bacterial culture to guide antimicrobial therapy.

Doctor_English: Is the skin rash only in areas exposed to the sun, or does it also appear in covered areas?
Patient_Yoruba: Àpá náà wà nínú ojú mi, àpá mi, àti ọrùn mi nìkan. Àwọn ibi tí aṣọ ń bò kò ní àpá náà, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọmọ aláṣẹ ìwòsàn ṣe ṣàlàyé.
Clinical_Translation_English: Photodistributed rash confined to sun-exposed areas of the face, arms, and neck with sparing of covered skin, consistent with a photodermatosis such as polymorphous light eruption, lupus erythematosus, or drug-induced photosensitivity.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any hair loss alongside the skin changes, either on your scalp or body?
Patient_Yoruba: Irun orí mi ń bọ̀ lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀ lẹ́yìn ìwẹ̀. Mo tún rí àwọn ibi tó ṣòòfà lórí orí mi tí irun kò hù mọ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Diffuse hair shedding with patchy alopecia on the scalp, occurring in the context of skin changes, raising concern for alopecia areata, discoid lupus erythematosus, or secondary syphilis.

Doctor_English: Has the affected skin area become thickened or leathery from repeated scratching?
Patient_Yoruba: Àwọ ara ní àyíká ìdọ̀tí mi ti di lile àti kíki nítorí mo ti ń gbá a fún oṣù mẹ́ta.
Clinical_Translation_English: Lichenification of the perianal skin secondary to chronic scratching over three months, consistent with lichen simplex chronicus complicating a primary pruritic condition such as eczema or psoriasis.

Doctor_English: Do you have similar skin patches on your elbows, knees, or scalp?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, ìpínpẹ̀lú mi àti orúkún mi ní àwọn ẹ̀gbọ̀n tó bo àwọ̀ funfun tí ó gbẹ. Orí mi tún máa ń gbẹ.
Clinical_Translation_English: Psoriatic plaques on extensor surfaces of the elbows and knees with associated scalp involvement, constituting a classic distribution of plaque psoriasis requiring dermatological management.

Doctor_English: Have you ever had a similar skin condition before, and did anything particular trigger it?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ní àpá kan bẹ́ẹ̀ nígbà tí mo jẹ ọmọ ọdún mẹ́wàá. Ó dára lẹ́yìn oṣù kan. Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, ìbánújẹ́ ló máa ń jẹ́ kí èyí bẹ̀rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Childhood history of similar cutaneous eruption with spontaneous resolution, with the current episode associated with psychosocial stress, consistent with stress-triggered atopic eczema or psoriasis flare.

Doctor_English: Is the skin around the rash showing any darkening or lightening compared to your normal skin tone?
Patient_Yoruba: Àpá náà ti lọ, ṣùgbọ́n ibi tó wà ní báyìí ti di dúdú síwájú sí i ju awọ ara mi ìbílẹ̀ lọ.
Clinical_Translation_English: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation persisting after rash resolution, a common sequela in patients with darker Fitzpatrick skin types, requiring topical depigmenting agents and photoprotection.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any small red or purple spots that do not disappear when you press on them?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo rí àwọn ìwọ̀ pupa kéékèèké lórí ẹsẹ̀ mi méjèèjì tí kò ń lọ nígbà tí a bá tẹ àwọ ara wọn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Non-blanching petechiae bilaterally on the lower limbs, a critical finding indicating possible vasculitis, thrombocytopaenia, or meningococcaemia, requiring urgent full blood count and coagulation studies.

Doctor_English: Does your skin wound heal normally, or do you develop raised thick scars after any injury?
Patient_Yoruba: Gbogbo ọgbẹ́ tí mo gba máa ń di èpo tó ga àti tó le, tó sì ń tóbi ju ọgbẹ́ àkọ́kọ́ lọ. Ó ti jẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ rí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Keloid formation following all cutaneous injuries, with hypertrophic scarring exceeding wound margins, indicating a hereditary predisposition to abnormal wound healing common in individuals of African ancestry.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any yellow crusty patches around your child's nose and mouth?
Patient_Yoruba: Àwọn ẹ̀gbọ̀n àwọ̀ pupa-pupa tó gbẹ wà ní àyíká imu àti ẹnu ọmọ mi. Ó ń tàn sí ẹrẹ̀kẹ́ rẹ̀ báyìí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Honey-coloured crusted lesions around the nasal and perioral regions with centrifugal spread in a child, the pathognomonic presentation of impetigo contagiosa caused by Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes.

Doctor_English: Does your skin become very dry and scaly in the harmattan season, and have you always had this?
Patient_Yoruba: Ní ìgbà òtútù, awọ ara mi máa ń gbẹ gan-an tó sì ń tán. Mo ti mọ̀ pé ara mi rí bẹ́ẹ̀ látìgbà mo wà lọ́mọdé.
Clinical_Translation_English: Lifelong seasonal xerosis with pronounced scaling during the harmattan season, consistent with ichthyosis vulgaris or severe atopic dry skin, requiring regular emollient therapy and humidity maintenance.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any white patches on your skin that have completely lost their colour?
Patient_Yoruba: Àwọn ìwọ̀ funfun ti fara hàn lórí ọwọ́ mi àti ẹ̀gbẹ́ ẹnu mi. Awọ ara wọn ti lọ pátápátá, wọ́n dín ju àwọ mi ìbílẹ̀ lọ.
Clinical_Translation_English: Depigmented macules on the dorsum of the hands and perioral region with complete loss of melanin, consistent with vitiligo — an autoimmune condition requiring dermatological evaluation and psychological support given its cosmetic impact.

Doctor_English: Have you been tested for HIV, and when was your last test done?
Patient_Yoruba: A ṣe ìdánwò HIV fún mi ní ọdún méjì sẹ́yìn, àbájáde rẹ̀ wà. Ṣùgbọ́n mo ti ní ìbálòpọ̀ tuntun lẹ́yìn ìdánwò náà.
Clinical_Translation_English: HIV test performed two years prior with a negative result, however subsequent unprotected sexual exposure necessitates repeat testing with a current window period-appropriate fourth-generation HIV antigen-antibody assay.

Doctor_English: Have you had any night sweats, persistent fever, and swollen glands in your neck or armpits?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ń gbọn lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀ ní alẹ́, ibà mi ń wáyé ní ìgbà-ìgbà, àti pé àwọn ẹ̀gbẹ̀ wà ní ọrùn mi tó ti ń pọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Constitutional B symptoms comprising night sweats, intermittent fever, and progressive cervical lymphadenopathy, raising concern for lymphoma, HIV-associated illness, or disseminated tuberculosis.

Doctor_English: Have you ever been diagnosed with or treated for tuberculosis in the past?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, mo ní ibà ẹ̀dọ̀fóró ní ọdún mẹ́fà sẹ́yìn. Mo pari oògùn náà fún oṣù mẹ́fà. Ṣùgbọ́n ikọ́ mi ti padà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Previous tuberculosis treatment completed six years ago with current recurrence of productive cough, raising concern for TB relapse, reinfection, or drug-resistant tuberculosis requiring sputum culture and drug sensitivity testing.

Doctor_English: Have you eaten any undercooked meat, unwashed vegetables, or untreated water recently?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo jẹ ẹran aguntan tí kò dára dáradára ní ọjọ́ mẹ́ta sẹ́yìn. Omi kanga ni mo máa ń mu ní abúlé wa.
Clinical_Translation_English: Recent ingestion of undercooked mutton and consumption of untreated well water, raising concern for enteric infections including typhoid fever, brucellosis, non-typhoidal salmonellosis, or intestinal parasitosis.

Doctor_English: Have you recently returned from a rural area, and have you been bitten by any insects?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo padà wá ní ọjọ́ mẹ́wàá sẹ́yìn láti abúlé kan ní Ògùn. Ẹfọn ń jẹ mí lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀ níbẹ̀, mi ò sì mú oògùn ìdáàbòbò.
Clinical_Translation_English: Return from a rural area in Ogun State ten days prior with significant mosquito exposure and no malaria chemoprophylaxis, presenting within the incubation period for Plasmodium falciparum malaria.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any sores or ulcers in your genital area, with or without pain?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo rí ọgbẹ́ kan ní àyíká ìbò mi tó kò ń dùn rárá. Ó ti wà fún ọjọ́ mẹ́wàá, kò ń sàn.
Clinical_Translation_English: A painless genital ulcer of ten days duration without spontaneous healing, the classic presentation of primary syphilis (chancre) caused by Treponema pallidum, requiring serological testing and partner notification.

Doctor_English: Have you been in close contact with anyone diagnosed with typhoid fever recently?
Patient_Yoruba: Ẹ̀gbọ́n mi ní ibà tàìfọ́ọ̀dù ní oṣù kan sẹ́yìn. A jẹ oúnjẹ papọ̀ ní gbogbo ọjọ́ fún ọ̀sẹ̀ mẹ́ta.
Clinical_Translation_English: Prolonged close household contact with a confirmed typhoid case including shared meals over three weeks, placing the patient at high risk for Salmonella typhi infection through faeco-oral transmission.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any yellowing of your eyes, dark urine, or pale stools alongside your fever?
Patient_Yoruba: Ojú mi ti di pàálu, ito mi di dúdú bí kọfì, àti ìgbẹ́ mi ti di funfun-funfun. Ibà mi ti wà fún ọjọ́ mẹ́wàá.
