Doctor_English: Have you had any painless skin ulcer with a clearly defined border that started as a small painless swelling?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọgbẹ́ kan wà lórí apá mi tó bẹ̀rẹ̀ bíi èso kéékèéké. Ó yà sí ọgbẹ́ tó ní ẹ̀bẹ́ tó gbẹ. Kò dùn rárá fún oṣù mẹ́ta.
Clinical_Translation_English: Painless necrotic ulcer with well-defined undermined edges evolving from a painless subcutaneous nodule over three months, the classic presentation of Buruli ulcer caused by Mycobacterium ulcerans requiring wound swab PCR and rifampicin-clarithromycin therapy.

Doctor_English: Have you ever been treated for river blindness, and do you live near a fast-flowing river?
Patient_Yoruba: A fún wa ní oògùn ivermectin ní ọdún kan sẹ́yìn ní abúlé wa. A ń gbé nitòsí odo tó ń sáré. Ojú mi ti ń rọ díẹ̀díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Progressive visual deterioration in a patient from a community near fast-flowing water receiving mass ivermectin distribution, raising concern for onchocerciasis with ocular involvement requiring slit-lamp examination and skin snip for microfilariae.

Doctor_English: Have you had any episodes of high fever with severe joint pain that prevented you from moving your joints?
Patient_Yoruba: Ibà tó ga àti irora ìpín tó le wáyé ní ìgbà kan. Mo kò lè gbe àwọn ìpín mi fún ọjọ́ mẹ́fà. Ó bẹ̀rẹ̀ lẹ́yìn ojú ọjọ́ ìkọlẹ̀ inú igbó.
Clinical_Translation_English: Acute febrile polyarthritis with severe joint immobility following bush exposure, consistent with chikungunya fever or o'nyong-nyong fever, both arboviral infections endemic in West Africa requiring viral serology and supportive management.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any thickening or scaling of your skin particularly on your legs, associated with itching?
Patient_Yoruba: Awọ ara ẹsẹ̀ mi méjèéjì ti ń gbẹ àti dín kù bíi ẹní. Ín-ín ń ṣe mí, pàápàá ní alẹ́. Ó ti jẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ fún ọdún méjì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Bilateral lower limb lichenification with pruritus and ichthyosis-like scaling over two years in a patient from an onchocerciasis-endemic area, features of chronic onchocercal skin disease requiring skin snip examination and ocular assessment.

Doctor_English: Have you ever been diagnosed with sleeping sickness or been tested for it after travelling to certain areas?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ṣiṣẹ́ ní igbó Benue fún oṣù mẹ́fà. Mo ti ní ìbà àti orí fífọ̀ lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀. Mo ti di sùúrù-sùúrù ní ọjọ́-ọjọ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Progressive somnolence, headache, and fever in a patient with forest exposure in a tsetse fly-endemic region, raising concern for African trypanosomiasis requiring blood film examination, lymph node aspiration, and lumbar puncture to stage the disease.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any skin patches that have lost sensation, with thickening of the nerves you can feel under the skin?
Patient_Yoruba: Àwọn ibi kan lára mi kò ní ìmọ̀ rárá nígbà tí a bá fi abẹ́rẹ̀ gún wọn. Mo tún lè fọwọ́ kan iṣan ara tó gbẹ ní ẹ̀gbẹ́ ọwọ́ mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Anaesthetic skin patches with palpable peripheral nerve thickening, the pathognomonic clinical combination of leprosy requiring slit skin smear, skin biopsy, and WHO multi-drug therapy for paucibacillary or multibacillary disease classification.

Doctor_English: Have you been passing blood in your urine without pain, particularly after swimming in lake or river water?
Patient_Yoruba: Ito mi máa ń ní ẹjẹ̀ nínú rẹ̀ ní ìgbà-ìgbà, kò ń dùn rárá. Mo máa ń wẹ nínú adágún omi ní abúlé wa.
Clinical_Translation_English: Painless terminal haematuria in a patient with freshwater lake exposure, the classic presentation of Schistosoma haematobium infection, requiring urine microscopy for ova after midday, and praziquantel treatment.

Doctor_English: Have you ever had a painless sore on your genitals that healed on its own, followed later by a rash all over your body?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọgbẹ́ kéékèéké kan wà ní ìdì mi ní ọdún kan sẹ́yìn tó sàn fúnra rẹ̀. Lẹ́yìn ọsù méjì, àpá tán gbogbo ara mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Primary syphilitic chancre with spontaneous healing followed by secondary syphilis rash, the classic two-stage progression of Treponema pallidum infection requiring RPR and TPHA serology, with penicillin G treatment and partner notification.

Doctor_English: Have you been having recurrent fevers every two or three days with shaking chills and sweating?
Patient_Yoruba: Ibà mi máa ń wáyé lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́ta ní ọ̀sẹ̀ kan pẹ̀lú ìgbọn àti ríru ẹjẹ̀ tó burú. Lẹ́yìn náà mo máa ń gbó ó sì ń rọ fún ọjọ́ kan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Tertian or quartan fever pattern with rigors, sweating, and interspersed well periods, highly characteristic of Plasmodium vivax or malariae infection requiring thick and thin blood film, rapid diagnostic test, and chloroquine or artemisinin therapy.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any rough raised patches on your skin around your ankles and shins that are very itchy?
Patient_Yoruba: Àwọn ẹ̀gbọ̀n tó gbẹ àti ga wà ní àyíká ẹsẹ̀ ìsàlẹ̀ mi àti ìhà àtẹ̀lẹ ẹsẹ̀ mi. Ín-ín ń ṣe mí gan-an, pàápàá ní alẹ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Hyperkeratotic pruritic papules in a gaiter distribution around the ankles and shins, consistent with nodular prurigo or chronic onchocercal skin disease, requiring skin snip examination and consideration of ivermectin therapy.

Doctor_English: Is the newborn feeding well, and how many times have they fed in the last twenty-four hours?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ tuntun mi jẹ ọmu lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́fà ní àárọ̀ ìmọ́lẹ̀ ṣùgbọ́n kò ní agbára láti mu lẹ́yìn ìrọlẹ́. Ó ti dẹ́kun sọkún.
Clinical_Translation_English: Reduced feeding frequency with loss of vigour and absent cry in a neonate, constituting danger signs of early neonatal sepsis or hypoglycaemia requiring immediate blood glucose, septic screen, and empirical antibiotic therapy.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed any yellowing of your newborn's skin or eyes within the first twenty-four hours of life?
Patient_Yoruba: Awọ ara ọmọ mi di pàálu ní ọjọ́ àkọ́kọ́ lẹ́yìn ìbímọ. Ojú rẹ̀ tún di pàálu. A tíì kúrò ilé ìwòsàn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Jaundice appearing within the first twenty-four hours of life is pathological until proven otherwise, requiring urgent serum bilirubin, blood group, Coombs test, and phototherapy initiation to prevent kernicterus.

Doctor_English: Has the baby passed their first stool, and what colour was it?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi kò tíì ṣe ìgbẹ́ kankan fún wákàtí mẹ́ẹ̀ẹ́dọ́gbọ̀n lẹ́yìn ìbímọ. Mo ti ń rọ ara mi, ìdílé mi sọ pé ó máa ń gba àkókò.
Clinical_Translation_English: Failure to pass meconium within forty-eight hours of birth, which excludes normal meconium passage in the first twenty-four hours, raises concern for Hirschsprung's disease, meconium ileus in cystic fibrosis, or imperforate anus requiring urgent neonatal surgical assessment.

Doctor_English: Is the umbilical cord stump dry and healing, or is there any redness, swelling, or discharge around it?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìkún ìfun ọmọ mi ń jáde omi tó ní òórùn búburú fún ọjọ́ méjì. Ó pupa ní àyíká rẹ̀ àti wú díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Malodorous umbilical discharge with periumbilical erythema and swelling in a neonate, indicating omphalitis — a potentially life-threatening bacterial infection that can progress to necrotising fasciitis or sepsis, requiring urgent systemic antibiotics and surgical assessment.

Doctor_English: Have you noticed the baby's fontanelle bulging outward, and is the baby very irritable or difficult to wake?
Patient_Yoruba: Orí ọmọ mi ń wu ní ibi tó rọ̀, ó sì ń kígbe tó ní ìgbà tí a bá fọwọ́ kan rẹ̀. Ó ṣòro láti jí i dìde láti jẹun.
Clinical_Translation_English: Bulging fontanelle with high-pitched cry and lethargy in a neonate, the classic triad of bacterial meningitis or raised intracranial pressure, constituting a neonatal emergency requiring immediate blood cultures, lumbar puncture, and empirical intravenous antibiotics.

Doctor_English: Has anyone in your family been told they carry the sickle cell trait, and has your newborn been tested?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọkọ mi àti èmi jẹ́ àwọn tó ń gbé àrùn ẹ̀jẹ̀ ààyọ. Ọmọ tuntun wa kò tíì ṣe ìdánwò. A dúró de ọjọ́ mẹ́ta sẹ́yìn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Both parents are confirmed sickle cell trait carriers, giving a twenty-five percent probability of sickle cell disease in the newborn. Newborn haemoglobin electrophoresis screening should be performed immediately to enable early prophylactic penicillin and parental counselling.

Doctor_English: Is the baby breathing normally, and have you noticed any grunting, flaring of the nostrils, or chest retractions?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi ń ṣe ìró kéékèéké nígbà tí ó bá ń mí. Imu rẹ̀ tún ń ìlà nígbà kọ̀ọ̀kan. A bímọ rẹ̀ ṣáájú àsìkò.
Clinical_Translation_English: Expiratory grunting, nasal flaring in a preterm neonate, the clinical signs of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome secondary to surfactant deficiency, requiring immediate CPAP or mechanical ventilation and exogenous surfactant administration.

Doctor_English: Has the baby been having any jerking movements of the arms or legs that you cannot stop by holding the limb?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi ń gbọn ní apá àti ẹsẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ìgbà-ìgbà. Nígbà tí mo bá di apá rẹ̀, ó tẹ̀síwájú. Ó ń wo ibi kan pẹ̀lú ojú rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Non-suppressible neonatal seizures with tonic-clonic limb movements and tonic eye deviation, requiring urgent blood glucose, calcium, electrolytes, and septic screen, with electroencephalography and phenobarbitone loading for confirmed seizures.

Doctor_English: Was the baby born on time, and did they cry immediately after birth?
Patient_Yoruba: A bímọ ọmọ mi ọ̀sẹ̀ mẹ́ta ṣáájú àsìkò. Kò sọkún lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀ lẹ́yìn ìbímọ. Wọ́n ṣiṣẹ́ lórí rẹ̀ fún ìṣẹ́jú díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Preterm birth with delayed spontaneous cry requiring resuscitative intervention, indicating birth asphyxia and prematurity as concurrent risk factors for neurodevelopmental sequelae, intraventricular haemorrhage, and respiratory distress syndrome requiring neonatal intensive care.

Doctor_English: Has the baby been gaining weight appropriately since birth, and how is the feeding going at home?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi ti pàdánù ìwọ̀ ara rẹ̀ láti ìgbà ìbímọ. Ó kò ń mu ọmu dáradára àti mi ò mọ̀ bóyá ọmú mi ń jáde tó.
Clinical_Translation_English: Neonatal weight loss beyond the acceptable ten percent threshold from birth weight with poor latch and maternal uncertainty about milk supply, indicating the need for immediate lactation support, infant weight monitoring, and supplementary feeding assessment.

Doctor_English: Have you been given any physiotherapy after your stroke, and have you been able to continue the exercises at home?
Patient_Yoruba: A fún mi ní àṣà ìdárayá lẹ́yìn ìjàǹbá ọpọlọ mi ní ilé ìwòsàn. Ṣùgbọ́n mi ò tẹ̀síwájú ní ilé nítorí mi ò ní ẹni tó ràn mí lọ́wọ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Post-stroke physiotherapy commenced in hospital with home exercise programme not maintained due to absence of a carer, indicating the need for community rehabilitation services, home visit physiotherapy, and carer training to optimise neurological recovery.

Doctor_English: Have you been using your walking aid correctly, and does it give you enough support?
Patient_Yoruba: Dọ́kítà fún mi ní ọpá ìrìn ṣùgbọ́n mi ò mọ̀ bí a ṣe ń lo rẹ̀ dáradára. Mo máa ń fi ọ̀tún mu, ṣùgbọ́n ẹsẹ̀ tó àìlera ni ó wa ní apá ọ̀tún.
Clinical_Translation_English: Incorrect walking aid use with the stick on the same side as the weak limb, reducing its effectiveness and increasing fall risk, requiring immediate occupational therapy gait re-education and assessment for the appropriate level of walking aid.

Doctor_English: Have you been able to return to any of your previous activities since your injury, such as cooking or dressing?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ti padà sí jíjẹun fúnra mi ṣùgbọ́n sísè tún ṣòro. Mo ń rí ìṣòro láti wọ aṣọ mi fúnra mi, pàápàá ní àpá mi tó àìlera.
Clinical_Translation_English: Partial functional recovery with independent feeding but persistent difficulty with cooking and dressing requiring upper limb occupational therapy, adaptive equipment provision, and kitchen modification assessment to maximise independence.

Doctor_English: Have you been experiencing any phantom limb pain since your amputation?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo máa ń ní irora ní ibi ẹsẹ̀ mi tó ti gé kúrò, bíi ẹni pé ó ṣì wà. Ó máa ń bani lóru ní alẹ́ àti ń dí mí lọ́wọ́ ìsùn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Phantom limb pain causing nocturnal sleep disruption post-amputation, a neuropathic pain syndrome requiring mirror therapy, graded motor imagery, gabapentinoid pharmacotherapy, and psychological support for body image adaptation.

Doctor_English: Have you been able to communicate effectively since your stroke, and has your speech improved?
Patient_Yoruba: Lẹ́yìn ìjàǹbá ọpọlọ mi, sísọ ọ̀rọ̀ ti ṣòro. Mo mọ̀ ohun tí mo fẹ́ sọ ṣùgbọ́n àwọn ọ̀rọ̀ kò jáde dáradára. Ó ti yẹ̀ díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Expressive aphasia with preserved comprehension following stroke showing partial spontaneous improvement, requiring formal speech and language therapy assessment to distinguish Broca's aphasia from other aphasia subtypes and guide targeted rehabilitation.

Doctor_English: Has your child been given any assistive devices for their disability, such as a wheelchair or hearing aids?
Patient_Yoruba: A fún ọmọ mi ní kẹ̀kẹ̀ àtọwọ́dọwọ́ ṣùgbọ́n ó ti fọ́ fún oṣù mẹ́fà. Ilé ẹ̀kọ́ rẹ̀ kò ní àyè fún ọmọ tó ń lo kẹ̀kẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Broken wheelchair for six months in a child with a physical disability attending a school without accessible infrastructure, indicating failure of assistive device maintenance and the intersection of disability with educational access barriers requiring urgent disability services intervention.

Doctor_English: Have you been experiencing any pressure sores or skin breakdown from sitting or lying in one position for long?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọgbẹ́ tó dùn wà ní ìdí mi àti ẹ̀gbẹ́ orúkún mi nítorí mo jókòó ní kẹ̀kẹ̀ fún wákàtí pípẹ́ lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Pressure ulcers at the sacrum and lateral knee from prolonged wheelchair positioning without adequate pressure relief, requiring wound assessment, pressure-relieving cushion, repositioning schedule, and nutrition optimisation for wound healing.

Doctor_English: Since your spinal cord injury, have you been having any difficulty knowing when your bladder or bowel is full?
Patient_Yoruba: Láti ìjàǹbá ẹ̀hin mi, mi ò lè mọ̀ nígbà tí ìkùn ito mi kún. Mo ti ṣe ito nínú àṣọ mi lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́ta ní ọ̀sẹ̀ kan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Neurogenic bladder with loss of bladder sensation and three episodes of urinary incontinence in one week following spinal cord injury, requiring urodynamic assessment, intermittent self-catheterisation training, and continence specialist referral.

Doctor_English: Have you had your blood pressure checked in the last year, and do you know what the numbers mean?
Patient_Yoruba: Dọ́kítà wọn ẹjẹ̀ mi ní ọdún méjì sẹ́yìn. Wọ́n sọ àwọn nọ́mbà náà ṣùgbọ́n mi ò gba ìtúmọ̀ wọn. Mi ò mọ̀ bóyá wọn dára tàbí bẹ́ẹ̀kọ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Blood pressure measured two years ago without patient education on the values or their significance, a health literacy gap that prevents informed self-management and highlights the need for structured cardiovascular risk communication.

Doctor_English: Have you ever had your blood glucose tested, and do you know your risk factors for diabetes?
Patient_Yoruba: Mi ò tíì ṣe ìdánwò suga ẹjẹ̀ kankan rí. Bàbá mi ní àrùn suga, mo sì ń gbé ìgbé ayé tó ń jókòó nìkan.
Clinical_Translation_English: No prior blood glucose testing in a patient with a first-degree family history of diabetes and a sedentary lifestyle, constituting two major modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors warranting immediate fasting glucose and HbA1c screening.

Doctor_English: Have you had a stool test for parasites or blood done recently, and have you been told the result?
Patient_Yoruba: Mi ò tíì ṣe ìdánwò ìgbẹ́ kankan rí bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé dọ́kítà sọ fún mi ní ọdún kan sẹ́yìn pé kí n ṣe rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Unfulfilled stool examination request from one year ago for parasitic infection or occult blood, representing a lost diagnostic opportunity in a patient at risk for helminthiasis or early colorectal pathology requiring immediate stool microscopy and faecal occult blood test.

Doctor_English: Have you had your eyes tested for glaucoma, particularly given that you have a family history of it?
Patient_Yoruba: Ẹ̀gbọ́n mi ti di afọ́ nítorí àrùn ojú kan tí wọ́n pè ní glaucoma. Mi ò tíì ṣe ìdánwò ojú fún ìtẹ̀wọ́ inú ojú rí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Positive family history of glaucoma causing blindness in a sibling without personal intraocular pressure screening, indicating high genetic risk and the urgent need for tonometry, optic disc assessment, and visual field testing.

Doctor_English: Have you received your routine adult booster vaccinations, such as tetanus, in the last ten years?
Patient_Yoruba: Mi ò ṣe àjesara kankan rí lẹ́yìn ìgbà ìkọ́lé. Mo ń ṣiṣẹ́ ní oko tí àwọn ọgbẹ́ máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lẹ́ẹ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan.
Clinical_Translation_English: No adult booster vaccination in a farmer with regular skin injury risk and no tetanus immunisation since childhood, indicating complete loss of protective immunity and the urgent need for tetanus toxoid booster and wound management protocol education.

Doctor_English: Have you been told you are at high risk for heart disease, and has anyone calculated your ten-year risk?
Patient_Yoruba: Dọ́kítà kan sọ pé eewu ọkàn mi ga ṣùgbọ́n kò fi ẹ̀rọ kankan ṣe iṣiro rẹ̀. Ó sọ nìkan pé kí n máà jẹ ọ̀rá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Verbal cardiovascular risk communication without formal risk score calculation using validated tools such as Framingham or WHO risk charts, preventing accurate risk stratification and potentially leading to under- or over-treatment of modifiable risk factors.

Doctor_English: Have you ever had a colonoscopy or bowel cancer screening, given that you are over fifty?
Patient_Yoruba: Mi ò tíì ṣe ìdánwò ìfun kankan rí. Mo jẹ́ ọmọ ọdún àádọ́ta àti ọrin-ọdún. Kò sí ẹni tó sọ fún mi pé mo nílò rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: No colorectal cancer screening in a patient in their late fifties, well beyond the recommended initiation age of fifty years, representing a significant prevention gap requiring immediate faecal immunochemical test or colonoscopy depending on available resources.

Doctor_English: Have you been offered a diabetes prevention programme given your pre-diabetes result last year?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìdánwò mi ní ọdún kan sẹ́yìn fihàn pé suga mi wà ní etí. Dọ́kítà sọ pé kí n padà wá ṣùgbọ́n kò tọ́ka mi sí ètò kankan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Pre-diabetes identified one year ago without referral to a structured diabetes prevention programme, representing a missed opportunity for lifestyle intervention that can reduce progression to type two diabetes by up to fifty-eight percent.

Doctor_English: Have you had your kidneys checked with a urine test and blood test in the context of your long-term diabetes?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ti ní àtọ̀gbẹ̀ fún ọdún mẹ́wàá ṣùgbọ́n mi ò ṣe ìdánwò ẹ̀dọ̀ ito mi fún ọdún méjì. Dọ́kítà kò sọ fún mi pé a nílò rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Ten-year history of diabetes without renal surveillance for two years, creating a significant risk of undetected diabetic nephropathy, the leading cause of end-stage renal disease, requiring immediate urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio and estimated GFR measurement.

Doctor_English: Have you been told about lifestyle changes that can reduce your risk of a second heart attack?
Patient_Yoruba: Lẹ́yìn ìjàǹbá ọkàn mi, wọ́n fún mi ní oògùn ṣùgbọ́n kò sí ẹni tó sọ̀rọ̀ pẹ̀lú mi nípa oúnjẹ, ìdárayá, tàbí dídáwọ̀ sìgá dúró.
Clinical_Translation_English: Post-myocardial infarction secondary prevention lacking structured cardiac rehabilitation, dietary counselling, smoking cessation support, and exercise prescription, all of which are evidence-based interventions reducing recurrent event risk by up to twenty-five percent.

Doctor_English: Have you been taking any agbo or other local herbal drinks regularly, and how often?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo máa ń mu agbo ní gbogbo owúrọ̀ fún ọdún méjì. Ìyá mi ń ṣe rẹ̀. Ó ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ewé àti igi nínú rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Daily consumption of traditional herbal agbo preparation for two years without knowledge of constituents, creating significant risk for hepatotoxicity, heavy metal toxicity, and pharmacokinetic drug interactions if prescribed medications are co-administered.

Doctor_English: Have you visited any prayer house or spiritual home for healing before coming to the hospital?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo lọ sí ilé ìjọsìn fún ọjọ́ mẹ́wàá ṣáájú kí n tó wá. Wọ́n gbàdúrà fún mi àti fún mi ní omi mímọ́ láti mu. Àìsàn mi kò yẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Faith healing consultation for ten days prior to hospital presentation with consumption of blessed water of unknown composition, illustrating the frequent parallel use of spiritual and biomedical healthcare in Nigerian patients and the potential delay in diagnosis this creates.

Doctor_English: Have you been using any skin-lightening creams that were bought from the market without a prescription?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ń lo ìpàrà awọ ara tó ń dín àwọ mi kù fún ọdún mẹ́fà. Mo ra a ní ọjà Balogun. Àmì kankan kò wà lórí rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Six years of unlabelled market-purchased skin-lightening cream use, likely containing mercury or high-potency topical corticosteroids, creating risk for mercurial nephropathy, adrenal suppression, cutaneous atrophy, and paradoxical hyperpigmentation.

Doctor_English: Have you been under pressure from your family to have more children even though you feel your family is complete?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìdílé ọkọ mi fẹ́ kí n bí ọmọ tó pọ̀ jù. Mo ti bí mẹ́fà ṣùgbọ́n wọn sọ pé ọkùnrin kò tó. Ara mi ti rẹ̀ mí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Reproductive coercion from in-laws with six prior deliveries in a physically exhausted patient, highlighting the intersection of cultural pressures, women's reproductive autonomy, and maternal health risk requiring sensitive counselling and confidential family planning support.

Doctor_English: Has anyone in your household been treating malaria at home with over-the-counter drugs before coming here?
Patient_Yoruba: A ra oògùn ibà ní ọjà fún ọmọ mi ọjọ́ mẹ́ta ṣáájú kí a tó wá ilé ìwòsàn. Ibà náà kò parí rárá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Three days of self-purchased antimalarial therapy without parasitological confirmation, delaying accurate diagnosis and creating risk of partial treatment of severe malaria or treatment of a non-malarial febrile illness with inappropriate drugs.

Doctor_English: Have you been eating clay, chalk, or soil during this pregnancy, and how much per day?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, mo máa ń jẹ amọ̀ funfun tó wà ní ọjà ní ìgbà oyún mi. Mo jẹ ẹ bíi igbá kéékèéké ní ọjọ́ kan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Gestational pica with daily consumption of clay in pregnancy, an indicator of iron deficiency anaemia and zinc deficiency, requiring immediate haematological assessment and micronutrient supplementation while addressing the soil contamination risk for intestinal parasitosis.

Doctor_English: Have you had any experience with a baby dying in the first week of life, and do you know what caused it?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ tí mo bí kẹ́ta kú ní ọjọ́ méje lẹ́yìn ìbímọ. Wọ́n sọ pé ikùn rẹ̀ ń ṣòro. Kò sí àlàyé tó péye.
Clinical_Translation_English: Previous neonatal death on day seven from a possible surgical abdominal cause without adequate explanation, a significant obstetric history that may indicate a hereditary condition, structural anomaly recurrence risk, or perinatal care quality gap requiring detailed counselling.

Doctor_English: Have you been told that your child has sickle cell disease, and do you understand what this means for daily life?
Patient_Yoruba: A sọ fún wa pé ọmọ wa ní àrùn ẹ̀jẹ̀ ààyọ ní oṣù mẹ́fà sẹ́yìn. A kò mọ̀ ohun tó túmọ̀ sí fún ìgbé ayé rẹ̀ lọ́jọ́ gbogbo.
Clinical_Translation_English: Sickle cell disease diagnosis six months ago without structured education on daily management including prophylactic penicillin, malaria prevention, hydration, fever response protocol, and signs of acute crisis requiring emergency attendance.

Doctor_English: Have you been carrying your hospital card with you, and do you always bring it to every visit?
Patient_Yoruba: Kàdì ìwòsàn mi ti sọnu ní ọdún kan sẹ́yìn. Mi ò rà tuntun. Gbogbo àwọn ìbẹ̀wò mi ti wà lórí kàdì náà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Lost hospital card containing complete medical history without replacement, a significant barrier to continuity of care in a setting where records are card-based, requiring re-registration and emphasis on the importance of records in enabling safe, coordinated care.

Doctor_English: Have you been giving your child herbs to treat the fever before coming, and what was in them?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo fun ọmọ mi ní àgbo ewé tí ìyá mi mú wá láti abúlé ṣáájú kí a tó wá. Mi ò mọ̀ ohun tó wà nínú rẹ̀. Ó mu u fún ọjọ́ méjì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Two days of traditional herbal fever treatment in a febrile child with unknown herbal constituents prior to hospital presentation, potentially masking clinical features, delaying diagnosis of bacterial meningitis or severe malaria, and introducing unknown pharmacological interactions.

Doctor_English: Have you ever refused a blood transfusion before for religious or personal reasons, and is this still your wish?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo jẹ́ Ẹlẹ́rìí Jèhófà. Ìgbàgbọ́ mi kò jẹ́ kí n gba ẹjẹ̀ ẹlòmíràn. Mo ti kọ àjẹjẹ̀ lẹ́ẹ̀kan ní ilé ìwòsàn mìíràn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Jehovah's Witness patient with prior documented blood transfusion refusal on religious grounds, requiring respectful elicitation of current wishes with clear documentation, exploration of blood conservation alternatives, and advance decision-to-refuse treatment documentation.

Doctor_English: Have you been eating enough during Ramadan, and has this affected your medication schedule?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo ń àwẹ̀ fún oṣù Ramadan. Mo dáwọ̀ mu oògùn ìpàná ẹjẹ̀ mi nígbà ọjọ́ nítorí àwẹ̀. Mo máa ń mu wọn lẹ́ẹ̀mejì ní alẹ́ péré.
Clinical_Translation_English: Ramadan fasting with self-modified antihypertensive dosing without medical guidance, creating risk of inadequate blood pressure control during the day and relative overdose at night, requiring culturally sensitive medication timing counselling from a clinician.

Doctor_English: Have you been told to take iron tablets but find the smell or taste makes you feel sick?
Patient_Yoruba: Oògùn irin tí dọ́kítà fún mi ń mú inú mi ṣòro àti ríru. Mo dáwọ̀ mu lẹ́yìn ọjọ́ mẹ́ta. Ìkùn mi tún máa ń ṣòro.
Clinical_Translation_English: Iron supplementation-induced gastrointestinal intolerance causing self-discontinuation after three days, a common adherence barrier addressable by switching to a different iron salt, dose reduction, taking with food, or considering parenteral iron in cases of malabsorption or intolerance.

Doctor_English: Have you been told your child needs glasses, but you have not been able to afford them?
Patient_Yoruba: Dọ́kítà ojú sọ pé ọmọ mi nílò àwo ojú fún ọdún kan ṣùgbọ́n a kò ní owó fún rẹ̀. Ó ń jókòó iwájú kíláàsì àti tún ń ṣàṣeyọrí kéré ní ìkọ́lé.
Clinical_Translation_English: One year of uncorrected refractive error in a child with documented spectacle prescription and affordability barrier, resulting in educational underperformance and visual strain, indicating the need for vision welfare programme referral or low-cost spectacle provision.

Doctor_English: Have you been using the same syringe more than once to inject your insulin, and how are you storing it?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo máa ń lo abẹ́rẹ̀ insulin mi lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́rin ṣáájú kí n tó pàárọ̀ rẹ̀. A fi insulin mi pamọ́ ní ìta ilé wa nítorí àpótí ìtọ́jú tutù kò ṣiṣẹ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Repeated use of insulin syringes and improper ambient temperature storage of insulin in a resource-limited setting, causing syringe blunting with increased injection pain, contamination risk, and insulin degradation leading to erratic glycaemic control.

Doctor_English: Have you had any difficulty accessing your regular medications during the fuel scarcity or strikes?
Patient_Yoruba: Nígbà ìdẹ́kun ọkọ̀ àti aawọ epo tó kọjá, mi ò lè de ilé ìwòsàn fún oṣù kan. Mo pari oògùn mi àti kò rà tuntun.
Clinical_Translation_English: One month of medication interruption during a period of industrial action and fuel scarcity, illustrating how systemic socioeconomic disruptions directly impact medication adherence and chronic disease management in the Nigerian healthcare context.

Doctor_English: Have you been giving your child undiluted adult medications because you could not find or afford the children's version?
Patient_Yoruba: Mi ò rí oògùn ọmọ ní ọjà àdúgbò wa. Ìdílé mi sọ pé kí n fún ọmọ mi ní ìdajì tábùlẹ́tì àgbàlagbà. Mo ṣe bẹ́ẹ̀ fún ọjọ́ méjì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Two days of adult medication administered at halved doses to a child due to paediatric formulation unavailability, creating significant risk of dosing error, toxicity, or subtherapeutic treatment — a recognised consequence of essential medicines supply chain gaps in Nigeria.

Doctor_English: Have you been fasting with dry mouth or without water as part of your pre-operative preparation?
Patient_Yoruba: Wọ́n sọ fún mi pé kí n má mu ohunkóhun láti òru àná. Mi ò mu omi tàbí jẹun. Mo ti ń gbẹ ẹnu mi fún wákàtí mẹ́rìnlá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Prolonged pre-operative fasting exceeding current enhanced recovery protocols, with water restriction beyond the recommended two-hour clear fluid fast, creating risk of dehydration, hypotension on induction, and metabolic stress, requiring updated fasting instructions.

Doctor_English: Have you been sharing your diabetes medications with a family member who cannot afford their own?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo máa ń fún ọkọ mi ní ìdajì oògùn suga mi nítorí ó kò ní owó fún tìrẹ. Àwọn oògùn wa yàtọ̀ síra wọn ṣùgbọ́n a gbà pé wọn jọra.
Clinical_Translation_English: Medication sharing between spouses with different diabetes regimens due to financial constraint, creating risk of wrong drug administration, subtherapeutic dosing for both patients, and potential adverse drug reactions from an inappropriate formulation.

Doctor_English: Have you been told you need dialysis, and have you been able to access it regularly?
Patient_Yoruba: Dọ́kítà sọ fún mi pé mo nílò ìtọ́jú ẹ̀dọ̀ ito ní ọdún kan sẹ́yìn. Mo ti ṣe rẹ̀ lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́ta nìkan nítorí owó. Ẹsẹ̀ mi ti ń wú síwájú sí i.
Clinical_Translation_English: End-stage renal disease with only three dialysis sessions completed in one year due to financial barriers against a recommended three sessions per week, resulting in progressive uraemia manifesting as worsening oedema and requiring urgent renal support programme review.

Doctor_English: Have you been drinking sachet water, tap water, or borehole water, and has it been treated?
Patient_Yoruba: A máa ń mu omi borehole tí àdúgbò wa ń pin. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ènìyàn lo ọmọ orin kan ṣoṣo. A kò máa ń ṣe àlẹ̀ rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Shared untreated borehole water consumption without purification in a densely populated community, a high-risk scenario for waterborne disease transmission including cholera, typhoid fever, and hepatitis A, requiring community water quality testing and household treatment education.

Doctor_English: On a scale of one to ten, how would you rate the severity of your headache right now?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora orí mi wà ní mẹ́sàn-án lára ọgọ́rùn-ún. Ó le tó jẹ́ kí n máà lè ṣiṣẹ́ rárá. Mo ní ìjọba ojú tún.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe headache rated nine out of ten with associated photophobia and inability to function, constituting a high-severity presentation requiring urgent exclusion of subarachnoid haemorrhage, meningitis, and hypertensive emergency.

Doctor_English: How severe is the itching on a scale of one to ten, and does it affect your ability to concentrate?
Patient_Yoruba: Ín-ín mi wà ní ẹjọ lára ọgọ́rùn-ún. Ó ń dẹ́kun iṣẹ́ mi àti ìsùn mi. Mo kò lè jókòó ní ibi ìpàdé.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe pruritus rated eight out of ten with occupational and sleep impairment, indicating a significant dermatological or systemic condition requiring urgent investigation including liver function tests, renal function, thyroid profile, and full blood count.

Doctor_English: How severe is your dizziness — does it prevent you from standing, or can you still move around if you are careful?
Patient_Yoruba: Orí mi yípo tó jẹ́ kí n máà lè dúró tàbí rìn. Mo ní láti di ohun kan mú nígbà gbogbo. Ó wà ní mẹ́sàn-án lára ọgọ́rùn-ún.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe vertigo rated nine out of ten causing complete inability to ambulate without support, indicating acute vestibular failure, posterior circulation stroke, or Ménière's crisis requiring urgent neurological assessment and fall prevention measures.

Doctor_English: How severe is the fatigue — are you able to carry out any activity at all, or are you confined to bed?
Patient_Yoruba: Àárẹ̀ mi le tó jẹ́ kí n máà lè dìde lórí ibùsùn mọ́. Mo wà ní ibùsùn fún ọjọ́ mẹ́fà. Àwọn ọmọ mi ń mu oúnjẹ wá fún mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Completely bedridden fatigue for six days with total dependence for feeding, indicating profound systemic illness such as severe anaemia, malignancy, cardiac failure, or sepsis requiring comprehensive evaluation and urgent supportive care.

Doctor_English: On a scale of one to ten, how severe is the burning when you pass urine, and is it getting worse?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora iná ní ìtọ mi wà ní mẹ́jọ lára ọgọ́rùn-ún. Ó ti ń burú sí i ní gbogbo ọjọ́ fún ọ̀sẹ̀ kan. Ibà tún ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ọjọ́ méjì sẹ́yìn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Progressive dysuria rated eight out of ten over one week with recent fever onset, indicating ascending urinary tract infection progressing toward pyelonephritis, requiring urgent urine culture, blood cultures, and empirical antibiotic therapy.

Doctor_English: How severe is the chest tightness — does it stop you from talking in full sentences or climbing stairs?
Patient_Yoruba: Àyà mi dín mọ́ tó jẹ́ kí n máà lè pari ọ̀rọ̀ kan láìsinmi. Ìtẹ̀lọ́ọ̀dọ̀ kan jẹ ẹni lóru mi. Ó le fún mi ní mẹ́jọ.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe chest tightness rated eight out of ten causing inability to complete sentences and single-stair limitation, indicating critical cardiorespiratory compromise requiring immediate oxygen, ECG, cardiac biomarkers, and emergency cardiology assessment.

Doctor_English: How severe is the abdominal cramp right now, and does pressing on your abdomen make it worse?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora ikùn mi wà ní mẹ́wàá lára ọgọ́rùn-ún lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́. Nígbà tí a bá tẹ ikùn mi, irora náà ń burú lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀. Ìdọ̀tí mi tún kò ṣẹlẹ̀ fún ọjọ́ méjì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Maximum severity abdominal pain with rebound tenderness on palpation and absolute constipation, constituting a surgical emergency with peritonitis signs requiring immediate surgical assessment, intravenous access, and urgent operative planning.

Doctor_English: How severe is the neck pain — can you move your neck freely, or is any movement very painful?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọrùn mi ń dùn ní mẹ́sàn-án. Mi ò lè yi orí mi sí ẹ̀gbẹ́ kankan láìní irora tó le. Ibà tún wà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe neck pain rated nine out of ten with meningismus and fever, the clinical triad mandating immediate lumbar puncture after neuroimaging to exclude bacterial meningitis, and empirical antibiotic administration before investigation results are available.

Doctor_English: How severe is the back pain at its worst, and does lying flat or walking make any difference?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora ẹ̀hin mi le ní mẹ́jọ sí mẹ́sàn-án. Dùbúlẹ̀ pẹ̀tẹ́lẹ̀ ń mú ki ó burú. Ìjókòó àti tẹ̀ iwájú ń mú ki ó yẹ̀ díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe back pain rated eight to nine out of ten worsening in supine position and relieved by forward flexion, the positional pattern of inflammatory spondyloarthropathy such as ankylosing spondylitis, distinct from mechanical back pain which improves with rest.

Doctor_English: How severe is the joint swelling — is it preventing you from using the joint at all?
Patient_Yoruba: Orúkún mi wú tó jẹ́ kí n máà lè rìn rárá. Mo ń lo ọpá ìrìn fún ọjọ́ mẹ́ta. Irora rẹ̀ wà ní mẹ́wàá nígbà tí a bá tẹ̀ ẹ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe monoarthritis with complete functional loss of the knee joint requiring walking aid for three days and exquisite tenderness on palpation, constituting an orthopaedic emergency to exclude septic arthritis requiring urgent joint aspiration and Gram stain.

Doctor_English: How severe is the eye pain — does it wake you at night, and is the vision affected at the same time?
Patient_Yoruba: Ojú mi ń dùn ní mẹ́sàn-án tó ń jí mi ní alẹ́. Ojú mi tún ti di rórò àti ìrísí imọ̀lẹ̀ ń dán mi lójú gan-an.
Clinical_Translation_English: Nocturnal ocular pain rated nine out of ten with concurrent visual blurring and photophobia, constituting an ophthalmic emergency requiring same-day slit-lamp examination to exclude acute angle-closure glaucoma or anterior uveitis.

Doctor_English: How bad is the breathlessness at rest — can you say more than three or four words at a time?
Patient_Yoruba: Mí mi kúrú tó jẹ́ kí n sọ ọ̀rọ̀ méjì tàbí mẹ́ta péré ṣáájú kí n tó sinmi. Mo wà ní ibùsùn pẹ̀lú ìrọ̀lẹ́ mẹ́ta.
Clinical_Translation_English: Breathlessness at rest limiting speech to two to three words with three-pillow orthopnoea, indicating acute decompensated heart failure requiring emergency diuresis, oxygen, and echocardiography.

Doctor_English: How severe is the pain from the burn — and how large an area of the skin has been affected?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora ọgbẹ́ iná mi le ní mẹ́wàá. Apá mi méjèéjì àti àyà mi ni a jó. Awọ ara wa yẹ̀ nínú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ibi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Maximum severity burn pain with bilateral arm and anterior trunk involvement, representing a major burn injury exceeding fifteen percent total body surface area requiring immediate fluid resuscitation using the Parkland formula, analgesia, and burns unit transfer.

Doctor_English: How severe is the tooth pain at night, and does cold or hot food or drink make it immediately worse?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora eyín mi le ní mẹ́sàn-án ní alẹ́. Ohun tó tutù máa ń mú irora naa bàyìí lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀ tó ń pẹ́ fún ìṣẹ́jú díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Severe nocturnal dental pain with prolonged cold sensitivity rated nine out of ten, distinguishing irreversible pulpitis from reversible pulpitis and indicating the need for root canal treatment or extraction rather than simple analgesic management.

Doctor_English: How severe is the muscle pain after exercise — does it stop you from moving your limbs the following day?
Patient_Yoruba: Lẹ́yìn ìdárayá, iṣan ara mi ń dùn tó jẹ́ kí n máà lè gbé apá mi ní ọjọ́ tó tẹ̀le. Ó dúró fún ọjọ́ mẹ́ta.
Clinical_Translation_English: Exercise-induced delayed onset muscle soreness causing functional limitation for three days, beyond the typical forty-eight hour peak of normal DOMS, requiring creatine kinase measurement to exclude rhabdomyolysis, particularly if urine is dark.

Doctor_English: Does the pain come on at a specific time of day, and have you noticed any pattern to when it starts?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora mi máa ń wáyé ní àárọ̀ owúrọ̀ nìkan, ó máa ń rọ ní ọ̀sán. Ó ti jẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ fún oṣù mẹ́fà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Morning-predominant pain with afternoon improvement over six months, consistent with inflammatory arthritis where cytokine-driven overnight joint inflammation produces prolonged morning stiffness, distinguishing it from degenerative joint disease.

Doctor_English: Does the rash appear and disappear, and is there any pattern to when it gets worse?
Patient_Yoruba: Àpá mi máa ń fara hàn nígbà tí mo bá jẹ oúnjẹ kan pàtó tàbí nígbà tí mo bá bínú. Ó ń lọ lẹ́yìn wákàtí mẹ́fà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Episodic urticaria triggered by specific foods or emotional stress, resolving within six hours, consistent with acute allergic urticaria or cholinergic urticaria requiring allergen identification, antihistamine therapy, and anaphylaxis action plan.

Doctor_English: How long does each episode of palpitation last, and is there anything that reliably stops it?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọkàn mi máa ń lu yára yára fún ìṣẹ́jú mẹ́wàá sí ogún. Nígbà tí mo bá gbìyànjú láti kọ́ ẹ̀gbẹ́ pẹ̀lú ọwọ́ mi, ó máa ń dáwọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Paroxysmal tachycardia lasting ten to twenty minutes with vagal manoeuvre termination by carotid sinus massage, highly specific for atrioventricular nodal re-entrant tachycardia requiring electrophysiology study and consideration of catheter ablation.

Doctor_English: Is the abdominal pain present all the time, or does it come in waves and then completely go away?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà ń wáyé ní ìgbà-ìgbà, ó ń le gan-an fún ìṣẹ́jú díẹ̀ lẹ́yìn náà ó máa ń lọ pátápátá. Ó ń wáyé lẹ́ẹ̀mẹ́fà ní ọjọ́ kan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Colicky abdominal pain occurring in waves six times daily with complete interlude resolution, the pathophysiological hallmark of hollow viscus obstruction or smooth muscle spasm as seen in ureteric colic, biliary colic, or intestinal obstruction.

Doctor_English: Does the shortness of breath come on suddenly without warning, or do you get warning signs before it starts?
Patient_Yoruba: Mí mi kúrú máa ń wáyé lójijì láìsí ìkìlọ̀ kankan. Ó máa ń bani lóru nígbà tí ó ṣẹlẹ̀ nítorí mi ò mọ̀ ìgbà tó máa ń wáyé.
Clinical_Translation_English: Sudden onset dyspnoea without prodrome causing significant anxiety, consistent with pulmonary embolism, spontaneous pneumothorax, or paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia, each requiring distinct urgent investigation and management.

Doctor_English: Does the vision problem happen at a particular time of day, such as at dusk or in bright sunlight?
Patient_Yoruba: Ojú mi ń ṣòro ni ìrọlẹ́ nígbà tí ìmọ̀lẹ̀ bá ń dín. Ní ọ̀sán àti owúrọ̀, mo ń rí dáradára. Ó ti jẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ fún oṣù mẹ́ta.
Clinical_Translation_English: Isolated twilight visual impairment with preserved daytime and bright-light vision for three months, the classic presentation of night blindness (nyctalopia) secondary to vitamin A deficiency or retinitis pigmentosa requiring serum retinol and ERG assessment.

Doctor_English: Does the tremor happen only when you are trying to use your hands, or is it also present when your hands are at rest?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọwọ́ mi máa ń mì nígbà tí mo bá jókòó kí n sì fẹ́ gbe nǹkan. Nígbà tí mo bá ń lo wọn, ó máa ń dáwọ̀. Ó ń wáyé fún ọdún méjì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Resting tremor suppressed by voluntary movement of two years duration, the characteristic tremor of Parkinson's disease, distinguishing it from the action tremor of essential tremor which worsens with purposeful movement.

Doctor_English: Does the cough tend to be worse at a specific time — early morning, after meals, or late at night?
Patient_Yoruba: Ikọ́ mi máa ń burú jù ní àárọ̀ owúrọ̀ nígbà tí mo bá jí dìde àti lẹ́yìn oúnjẹ alẹ́. Ní ọ̀sán, ó máa ń yẹ̀ díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Morning and post-prandial cough exacerbation suggesting gastro-oesophageal reflux with nocturnal acid accumulation and post-meal reflux, a common non-infectious cause of chronic cough requiring pH-impedance monitoring and proton pump inhibitor trial.

Doctor_English: Is the swelling in your face worse in the morning and better by the evening, or the other way around?
Patient_Yoruba: Ojú mi máa ń wú ní àárọ̀ owúrọ̀ tó ń rọ ní ọ̀sán. Ní òrùlé ọjọ́, ẹsẹ̀ mi bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí wú dípò.
Clinical_Translation_English: Facial oedema predominating in the morning with dependent shift to lower limb by evening, consistent with nephrotic syndrome where hypoalbuminaemia causes positional oedema redistribution rather than purely dependent cardiac oedema.

Doctor_English: Does the chest pain come on within minutes of starting exercise, or only after prolonged activity?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora àyà mi wáyé ní ìṣẹ́jú méjì sẹ́yìn kí iṣẹ́ ìdárayá mi tó pé. Kò pẹ́ fún mi láti ń ṣeré tàbí gùn ìtẹ̀lọ́ọ̀dọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Angina of effort with very low exercise threshold causing symptoms within two minutes of activity onset, indicating severe fixed coronary artery stenosis or critical aortic stenosis requiring urgent cardiology evaluation and coronary angiography.

Doctor_English: Does the skin rash come and go with your menstrual cycle, worsening before your period?
Patient_Yoruba: Àpá mi máa ń burú ní ọjọ́ mẹ́ta ṣáájú oṣù mi àti yẹ̀ lẹ́yìn tí oṣù bá tàn. Ó ṣẹlẹ̀ ní ọ̀sẹ̀ mẹ́ta tó kọjá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Premenstrual flare of cutaneous symptoms with post-menstrual resolution, consistent with catamenial dermatosis, progesterone hypersensitivity, or premenstrual exacerbation of atopic eczema or acne, requiring hormonal assessment.

Doctor_English: Does the joint pain last all day without any variation, or does it improve as you move around?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora ìpín mi wà fún gbogbo ọjọ́ láìyẹ̀ rárá. Ìgbésẹ̀ kò ṣe ìyàtọ̀. Ó ń burú ní alẹ́ àti ní àárọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Constant joint pain without diurnal variation and nocturnal worsening, a pattern more consistent with inflammatory or malignant joint disease rather than mechanical pain which typically improves with rest and worsens with activity.

Doctor_English: Does the fever follow any pattern — does it go completely normal between episodes or stay slightly elevated?
Patient_Yoruba: Ibà mi máa ń wáyé fún ọjọ́ méjì, lẹ́yìn náà mo máa ń dára pátápátá fún ọjọ́ kan, lẹ́yìn náà ó padà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Relapsing fever pattern with complete afebrile intervals, the characteristic presentation of relapsing fever caused by Borrelia species, lymphoma with Pel-Ebstein fever, or periodic fever syndromes requiring blood film during the febrile phase.

Doctor_English: Does the urinary urgency happen at particular times, such as after drinking coffee or on hearing running water?
Patient_Yoruba: Nígbà tí mo bá gbọ́ ìró omi tó ń ṣàn, ìfẹ́ ito lójijì ń wáyé tí o ń ṣe mí ìṣòro. Kọfí tún ń mú ki ó buru.
Clinical_Translation_English: Sensory urgency triggered by auditory water cues and caffeine intake, classic precipitants of overactive bladder, indicating detrusor overactivity requiring bladder training, caffeine reduction, and anticholinergic or beta-3 agonist pharmacotherapy.

Doctor_English: Is the memory problem getting progressively worse month by month, or has it stayed at the same level?
Patient_Yoruba: Gbígbàgbé mi ti ń burú sí i ní ìgbà-ìgbà. Ní oṣù tó kọjá, ó burú sí i ju ọjọ́ àárọ̀ lọ gan-an. Àwọn ẹbí mi ti ṣàkíyèsí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Progressive cognitive deterioration noted by family with month-on-month worsening, distinguishing a neurodegenerative process such as Alzheimer's disease from stable mild cognitive impairment or depression-related pseudodementia.

Doctor_English: Did the weakness in your face start before or after the headache began?
Patient_Yoruba: Orí mi bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí fọ̀ ṣáájú kí ẹ̀gbẹ́ ojú mi tó rọ. Irora orí bẹ̀rẹ̀ lójijì, ojú mi sì rọ lẹ́yìn ìṣẹ́jú mẹ́wàá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Headache preceding facial weakness by ten minutes, a sequence supporting a vascular or migrainous aetiology rather than an isolated peripheral facial nerve palsy, requiring urgent brain imaging to exclude haemorrhagic stroke or hemiplegic migraine.

Doctor_English: Did the swelling start after you ate something specific, or did it come on without any obvious trigger?
Patient_Yoruba: Ète mi wú lẹ́yìn tí mo jẹ ẹ̀pà. Ó wáyé ní ìṣẹ́jú mẹ́wàá lẹ́yìn jíjẹ. A ti ṣe bẹ́ẹ̀ rí ṣùgbọ́n kò le bíi ìyẹn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Lip angioedema within ten minutes of peanut ingestion with prior milder reactions, indicating IgE-mediated peanut allergy with increasing severity, representing a potentially life-threatening pattern requiring adrenaline auto-injector prescription and allergy specialist referral.

Doctor_English: Did the rash appear before or after you started taking the new antibiotic?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo bẹ̀rẹ̀ oògùn àkóràn ní ọjọ́ Ìṣẹ́gun, àpá sì fara hàn ní ọjọ́ Ìṣẹ́gun. Ó ti ń tàn síwájú sí i.
Clinical_Translation_English: Drug rash with temporal onset two days after antibiotic initiation and progressive spread, consistent with a maculopapular drug eruption or urticaria requiring immediate antibiotic cessation, antihistamine therapy, and documentation of drug allergy.

Doctor_English: Did the confusion start before or after the fall, or is it impossible to say which came first?
Patient_Yoruba: Ó ṣubú lójijì, lẹ́yìn náà ní ìdàrúdàpọ̀. Ṣùgbọ́n ìyàwó rẹ̀ sọ pé ó ti ń sọ àwọn ọ̀rọ̀ àjèjì ṣáájú ìṣubú.
Clinical_Translation_English: Pre-fall confusion confirmed by the spouse, establishing that the cognitive impairment preceded rather than resulted from the fall, a critical temporal distinction that redirects the diagnosis toward a primary neurological or metabolic cause of the fall.

Doctor_English: Did the vision loss come on gradually over years, or was there a sudden change at a specific point?
Patient_Yoruba: Ojú mi ti ń rọ díẹ̀díẹ̀ fún ọdún mẹ́ta. Ṣùgbọ́n ní ọ̀sẹ̀ méjì sẹ́yìn, ìyẹn tó wà ṣẹ́ ṣubú lójijì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Gradual visual deterioration over three years with sudden acute loss two weeks ago superimposed on the baseline, suggesting a chronic condition such as glaucoma or diabetic retinopathy complicated by an acute event such as vitreous haemorrhage or retinal detachment.

Doctor_English: Did the leg pain start at the same time as the swelling, or did one come before the other?
Patient_Yoruba: Wiwú ẹsẹ̀ mi bẹ̀rẹ̀ ṣáájú irora. Ẹsẹ̀ mi wú ní owúrọ̀, irora sì bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ọ̀sán. Wọ́n wa papọ̀ ní báyìí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Oedema preceding pain in a lower limb with subsequent convergence of both symptoms, a sequence more consistent with deep vein thrombosis where venous obstruction causes swelling first and inflammatory pain follows, than with cellulitis where pain typically precedes swelling.

Doctor_English: Did the hearing loss happen suddenly in one ear or did it develop so slowly you barely noticed?
Patient_Yoruba: Gbígbọ́ etí òsì mi wọ lójijì kan ṣoṣo. Mo jí ní owúrọ̀ kan kò sì gbọ́ rárá. Ko jẹ́ díẹ̀díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Sudden unilateral sensorineural hearing loss on waking, classified as sudden sensorineural hearing loss when exceeding thirty decibels across three frequencies, constituting an otological emergency where high-dose corticosteroid initiation within seventy-two hours improves prognosis.

Doctor_English: Did the personality change come on after a period of stress, or gradually without any obvious trigger?
Patient_Yoruba: Àṣà ìwà ọkọ mi yí padà lẹ́yìn tí ó pada sí ilé lẹ́yìn àwọn ọdún iṣẹ́ ológun. Ko sí ìṣẹlẹ̀ pàtàkì kan tó ṣáájú rẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Post-military service personality change without a discrete precipitating incident suggesting subthreshold or undiagnosed post-traumatic stress disorder, moral injury, or traumatic brain injury from deployment, requiring detailed trauma history and neuropsychological assessment.

Doctor_English: Did the jaundice appear at the same time as the pain, or did the pain come first by several days?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora ìhà ọ̀tún ikùn mi bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ọjọ́ àárọ̀. Ojú mi di pàálu ní ọjọ́ kẹta. Ibà wáyé ní ọjọ́ kéjì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Charcot's triad with sequential onset of right upper quadrant pain, fever on day two, and jaundice on day three, the classic temporal presentation of acute ascending cholangitis requiring urgent biliary decompression, blood cultures, and intravenous antibiotics.

Doctor_English: Did the vomiting start before or after the diarrhoea, and by how many hours?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìgbẹ́ è gbọn bẹ̀rẹ̀ ṣáájú ìgbẹ́ gbuuru, bóyá wákàtí méjì ṣáájú. Ọjọ́ kanna ni wọ́n wáyé méjèéjì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Vomiting preceding diarrhoea by two hours, a sequence typical of viral gastroenteritis or Staphylococcal food poisoning where upper gastrointestinal symptoms dominate initially before lower GI involvement, distinct from cholera where profuse watery diarrhoea typically precedes vomiting.

Doctor_English: Can you describe the character of the pain — is it sharp like a knife, burning like fire, or dull like a pressure?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà dùn bíi ẹni tó ń gún mi pẹ̀lú abẹ́rẹ̀ tó gún jìnnà. Ko dùn bíi ìtẹ̀wọ́ tàbí iná. Ó ń wáyé lójijì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Sharp stabbing pain with acute onset distinguishing neuropathic or pleuritic aetiology from the pressure-like discomfort of ischaemia or the burning quality of inflammatory conditions, guiding analgesic selection and differential diagnosis.

Doctor_English: Does the headache feel like a tight band around your head, or is it throbbing like a pulse?
Patient_Yoruba: Orí mi ń dùn bíi ẹni pé igbó ti di àyíká rẹ̀ ó ń mú kí irora náà wà. Ko ń lu bíi ọkàn. Ó wà ní awọn ẹ̀gbẹ́ méjèéjì.
Clinical_Translation_English: Bilateral band-like non-pulsating headache, the characteristic quality of tension-type headache, distinguishing it from the unilateral pulsating throbbing of migraine, guiding therapy toward simple analgesics and stress management rather than triptans.

Doctor_English: Is the chest pain burning and coming up into your throat, or is it more like a crushing weight on your chest?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà dùn bíi iná tó ń jó ní àárín àyà mi tó ń rọ sí ọfun mi lẹ́yìn oúnjẹ. Ko dà bíi ìtẹ̀wọ́ rárá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Retrosternal burning radiating to the throat post-prandially, characteristic of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease, clearly distinguished from the crushing pressure quality of myocardial ischaemia, guiding toward endoscopy and acid suppression rather than cardiac investigation.

Doctor_English: Does the abdominal pain feel like a twisting cramp, or is it a constant dull ache without variation?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà yípo bíi ẹni tó ń fi ọwọ́ yi ikùn mi. Ó máa ń le, lẹ́yìn náà dín kù, lẹ́yìn náà le lẹ́ẹ̀kan sí i.
Clinical_Translation_English: Colicky twisting abdominal pain with waves of crescendo and decrescendo, the visceral pain character of smooth muscle spasm in ureteric colic, intestinal obstruction, or biliary colic, guiding toward antispasmodic therapy and targeted imaging.

Doctor_English: Is the leg pain cramping and relieved by rest, or is it a constant burning even when you are not moving?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora ẹsẹ̀ mi máa ń wa nígbà tí mo bá ń rìn, lẹ́yìn náà máa ń lọ nígbà tí mo bá dúró. Ko ń wà nígbà tí mo bá sinmi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Intermittent claudication with exertional cramp relieved by rest, the classic character of peripheral arterial disease, distinguished from the constant burning of peripheral neuropathy and the positional pain of venous claudication.

Doctor_English: Is the back pain a dull constant ache, or does it shoot down your leg like an electric shock?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora máa ń tẹ̀lé ọ̀nà iṣan ẹsẹ̀ mi bíi ìgbóná onítànná tó ń rọ láti ẹ̀hin wá sí ìpẹ̀sẹ̀ mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Radicular pain shooting along the sciatic nerve distribution from the back to the foot with an electric shock quality, neurogenic in character and distinguishing lumbar disc herniation with nerve root compression from non-specific mechanical back pain.

Doctor_English: Is the skin sensation an itching, burning, or something that feels like insects crawling under the skin?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìmọ̀ ara mi dà bíi ẹni tó ń ṣàn kánkán lábẹ́ awọ ara mi. Kò ń yọ, kò ń jó, ó dà bíi àwọn kòkòrò kéékèéké.
Clinical_Translation_English: Formication — the sensation of insects crawling under the skin — a specific dysaesthetic symptom associated with peripheral neuropathy, cocaine or alcohol withdrawal, delusional parasitosis, or polycythaemia vera requiring careful diagnostic differentiation.

Doctor_English: Is the cough a dry hacking sound, or does it produce phlegm, and what colour is the phlegm?
Patient_Yoruba: Ikọ́ mi ń jáde pẹ̀lú ìtọ́ àwọ̀ pàálu-alawọ. O tó ìwọ̀n ìkòkò kéékèéké ní ọjọ́ kan. Ó ti jẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ fún ọjọ́ mẹ́wàá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Purulent yellow-green sputum production of ten days duration indicating bacterial lower respiratory tract infection, guiding antibiotic therapy selection and sputum culture, while the volume and colour help distinguish acute bronchitis from pneumonia or bronchiectasis exacerbation.

Doctor_English: Is the palpitation a regular fast rhythm, or does it feel irregular like random extra beats?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọkàn mi ń lu ní ọ̀nà tí kò ṣe àmúlò, bíi ẹni tó ń fo ìgbà-ìgbà. Ó kò ní ìṣọ̀kan. Mo ń ní ìmọ̀lára pé ọkàn mi máa ń fo lẹ́ẹ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Irregular palpitation with a sensation of missed beats rather than a rapid regular rhythm, the subjective experience of ectopic beats or atrial fibrillation, requiring a twelve-lead ECG and twenty-four hour Holter monitor to characterise the arrhythmia.

Doctor_English: Does the joint pain feel deep inside the joint, or is it mainly on the surface of the skin over the joint?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà wà jíjìn nínú ìpín mi, kì í ṣe lórí awọ ara. Nígbà tí mo bá tẹ ìpín, irora ń wáyé nínú rẹ̀ gan-an.
Clinical_Translation_English: Deep articular pain on joint palpation distinguishing intra-articular pathology such as cartilage damage, synovitis, or crystal deposition from superficial periarticular conditions such as bursitis, tendinopathy, or cellulitis.

Doctor_English: Alongside the headache, have you had any vomiting, sensitivity to light, or stiff neck?
Patient_Yoruba: Pẹ̀lú orí fífọ̀ mi, mo ègbọn lẹ́ẹ̀mejì àti ìmọ̀lẹ̀ ń bani lójú mi. Ọrùn mi tún dì, mi ò lè tẹ orí mi wá siwájú.
Clinical_Translation_English: The meningism triad of headache, photophobia, and neck stiffness with vomiting constituting a meningitis emergency requiring immediate lumbar puncture after neuroimaging and empirical antibiotic and dexamethasone administration.

Doctor_English: With the chest pain, have you had sweating, nausea, or pain going into your jaw or left arm?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, mo gbó lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀ àti ríru wáyé papọ̀ pẹ̀lú irora àyà. Ẹ̀gbẹ́ ẹrẹ̀kẹ́ mi òsì tún ń dùn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Diaphoresis, nausea, and left jaw pain accompanying chest pain, constituting the autonomic and referred pain features of acute myocardial infarction, warranting immediate twelve-lead ECG, cardiac troponin, and primary PCI activation.

Doctor_English: With the fever, have you had any rash, joint pain, or eyes that are red and watery?
Patient_Yoruba: Pẹ̀lú ibà mi, àpá pupa fara hàn lórí ara mi, ìpín mi ń dùn, àti ojú mi ti di pupa àti omi ń jáde nínú.
Clinical_Translation_English: Fever with maculopapular rash, polyarthralgia, and conjunctivitis forming the classic triad of measles, dengue fever, or chikungunya — all endemic in Nigeria — requiring serology and notifiable disease reporting.

Doctor_English: Alongside the abdominal pain, have you had any shoulder tip pain or pain on breathing deeply?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, orí èjìká mi ọ̀tún ń dùn pẹ̀lú irora ikùn mi. Mí jìnnà tún ń mu irora buru sí i.
Clinical_Translation_English: Shoulder tip pain with abdominal pain and pleuritic exacerbation indicating diaphragmatic irritation from subphrenic pathology such as liver abscess, ruptured ectopic pregnancy, or free intraperitoneal gas, requiring urgent CT abdomen.

Doctor_English: With the urinary symptoms, have you had any loin pain, fever, or shaking chills?
Patient_Yoruba: Pẹ̀lú àìlera ito mi, irora wà ní ẹ̀gbẹ́ ẹ̀hin mi ọ̀tún, ibà, àti ìgbọn. Ó bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní àárọ̀ àná.
Clinical_Translation_English: Loin pain with fever and rigors accompanying urinary symptoms, the classic presentation of acute pyelonephritis, requiring blood and urine cultures before commencing intravenous antibiotics and assessing for urological complication.

Doctor_English: With the skin rash, have you had any joint swelling, mouth ulcers, or hair loss at the same time?
Patient_Yoruba: Papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àpá mi, ìpín mi wú, ọgbẹ́ wà nínú ẹnu mi, àti irun mi ti ń bọ̀ lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀. Gbogbo wọn wáyé papọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: The triad of rash, arthritis, oral ulceration, and alopecia occurring simultaneously constitutes a multisystem flare of systemic lupus erythematosus requiring ANA, anti-dsDNA, complement levels, and urine protein assessment.

Doctor_English: With the dizziness, have you had any hearing loss, ringing in your ear, or a feeling of fullness in the ear?
Patient_Yoruba: Pẹ̀lú orí yípo, etí òsì mi ń rò, gbígbọ́ mi ti dínkù ní ẹ̀gbẹ́ náà, àti ìmọ̀lára kún wà nínú etí mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Ménière's tetrad of vertigo, unilateral tinnitus, sensorineural hearing loss, and aural fullness on the same side, fulfilling diagnostic criteria for Ménière's disease requiring audiometry and ENT specialist management with dietary sodium restriction.

Doctor_English: With the cough, have you had any weight loss, night sweats, or swollen glands in your neck?
Patient_Yoruba: Pẹ̀lú ikọ́ mi, mo ti tẹ̀nìí, mo ń gbọn ní alẹ́, àti àwọn ẹ̀gbẹ̀ wà ní ọrùn mi tó ń pọ̀. Wọ́n wa papọ̀ fún oṣù mẹ́fà.
Clinical_Translation_English: The B symptom triad of weight loss, night sweats, and lymphadenopathy accompanying chronic cough over six months, strongly suggesting pulmonary tuberculosis or lymphoma, requiring chest X-ray, sputum AFB smear, and lymph node biopsy.

Doctor_English: With the leg swelling, have you had any shortness of breath when lying flat or waking up breathless at night?
Patient_Yoruba: Pẹ̀lú wiwú ẹsẹ̀ mi, mí mi kúrú nígbà tí mo bá dùbúlẹ̀ pẹ̀tẹ́lẹ̀ àti mo máa ń jí ní alẹ́ pẹ̀lú ìkúrú mímí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Peripheral oedema with orthopnoea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea constituting the classic triad of decompensated biventricular heart failure, requiring urgent diuresis, BNP measurement, echocardiography, and fluid restriction.

Doctor_English: With the confusion, have you had any fever, stiff neck, or sensitivity to light at the same time?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìdàrúdàpọ̀ ọkọ mi wáyé papọ̀ pẹ̀lú ibà, ọrùn tó dì, àti ìmọ̀lẹ̀ tó ń bani lójú. Gbogbo wọn bẹ̀rẹ̀ papọ̀ ní àárọ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Acute confusion with simultaneous fever, meningismus, and photophobia is bacterial meningitis until proven otherwise, requiring immediate blood cultures, empirical ceftriaxone and dexamethasone, and lumbar puncture as soon as safe to perform.

Doctor_English: Does anything make the headache better — lying in a dark room, taking pain relief, or sleeping?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìrọ̀lẹ́ ní yàrá òkùnkùn ń mú irora orí mi yẹ̀. Ìmọ̀lẹ̀ àti ariwo ń mú ki ó burú. Oògùn irora ń ràn mí lọ́wọ́ díẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Headache relieved by dark quiet environment and partially by analgesia with photophobia and phonophobia, fulfilling the diagnostic criteria for migraine without aura and guiding toward triptan therapy for acute management.

Doctor_English: Does the back pain get better or worse with prolonged sitting at a desk?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora ẹ̀hin mi ń burú sí i nígbà tí mo bá jókòó fún wákàtí mẹ́fà lórí àga iṣẹ́. Ìrìn àti ìdárayá ń mú ki ó yẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Mechanical back pain worsened by prolonged sitting and relieved by movement and exercise, consistent with lumbar disc derangement or facet joint dysfunction responding to physiotherapy, ergonomic correction, and anti-inflammatory analgesia.

Doctor_English: Does eating anything specific make the abdominal pain worse, and does fasting give you any relief?
Patient_Yoruba: Jíjẹ oúnjẹ tó ní ọ̀rá ń mú irora ikùn mi buru sí i lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀. Nígbà tí mo bá àwẹ̀, irora ń yẹ̀ ṣùgbọ́n ó ń padà nígbà tí mo bá jẹun.
Clinical_Translation_English: Post-prandial worsening with fatty food and fasting relief, the classic aggravating and relieving pattern of biliary colic from cholelithiasis, where bile flow stimulated by fatty meals precipitates gallbladder contraction against an obstructing calculus.

Doctor_English: Does the breathlessness improve when you lean forward with your hands on your knees?
Patient_Yoruba: Nígbà tí mo bá tẹ̀ iwájú mo sì fi ọwọ́ mi gbe orúkún mi, mí mi ń rọ díẹ̀. Ó jẹ́ ọ̀nà tí mo máa ń lo láti sinmi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Tripod positioning with orthopnoea relieved by forward lean, a compensatory posture that fixes the accessory muscles of respiration and is characteristic of severe COPD, acute asthma, or any cause of critical respiratory distress.

Doctor_English: Is the joint pain relieved by anti-inflammatory tablets, and if so, within how many hours?
Patient_Yoruba: Ibuprofen ń mú irora ìpín mi yẹ̀ ní wákàtí kan. Ó ń dára fún ọjọ́ kan, lẹ́yìn náà ó padà. Paracetamol kò ṣiṣẹ́ kankan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Rapid complete response to NSAIDs within one hour with poor paracetamol response, characteristic of inflammatory joint disease such as gout or rheumatoid arthritis, where prostaglandin-mediated synovial inflammation drives pain rather than structural joint damage.

Doctor_English: Does applying heat or cold to the area give you any relief, and which one works better?
Patient_Yoruba: Omi gbígbóná ń mú irora iṣan ara mi yẹ̀ sí i ju omi tutù lọ. Mo máa ń fi àpótí omi gbígbóná sí ibi náà ní alẹ́.
Clinical_Translation_English: Heat-responsive musculoskeletal pain indicating muscle spasm or chronic inflammatory joint disease where heat promotes muscle relaxation and analgesia, in contrast to acute joint injury or gout where cold application reduces acute inflammatory hyperaemia.

Doctor_English: Does the skin itching get worse after a hot shower or bath, and how quickly does it start?
Patient_Yoruba: Ín-ín mi ń bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ìṣẹ́jú méjì lẹ́yìn tí mo bá jáde nínú omi gbígbóná. Ó ń burú jù lẹ́yìn ìwẹ̀ gbígbóná.
Clinical_Translation_English: Aquagenic pruritus triggered within minutes of hot water exposure without a primary skin lesion, a distinctive pattern associated with polycythaemia vera and requiring full blood count with red cell mass measurement.

Doctor_English: Does the heartburn get worse when you bend over or lift heavy objects?
Patient_Yoruba: Nígbà tí mo bá tẹ̀ orí mi sísàlẹ̀ láti gba nǹkan, irora iná ń wáyé nínú àyà mi àti ọfun mi lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Positional gastro-oesophageal reflux worsened by forward bending due to increased intra-abdominal pressure overcoming the lower oesophageal sphincter, a hallmark of hiatus hernia-associated reflux requiring endoscopy and proton pump inhibitor therapy.

Doctor_English: Does the dizziness get worse when you turn over in bed or look up at the ceiling?
Patient_Yoruba: Nígbà tí mo bá yí ara mi padà ní ibùsùn tàbí gbé orí mi wò ní ẹ̀gbẹ́ ọ̀tún, orí mi máa ń yípo lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀ fún ìṣẹ́jú mẹ́wa.
Clinical_Translation_English: Positional vertigo triggered by specific head movements in bed and lasting under one minute, fulfilling the Dix-Hallpike diagnostic criteria for benign paroxysmal positional vertigo from canalith displacement, treated with the Epley repositioning manoeuvre.

Doctor_English: Does the cough stop when you drink cold water, or does cold air make it worse?
Patient_Yoruba: Omi tutù ń mú ikọ́ mi dáwọ̀ fún ìṣẹ́jú díẹ̀. Ṣùgbọ́n afẹ́fẹ́ tutù ní ìta ń mú ki ikọ́ mi le sí i.
Clinical_Translation_English: Cold water transiently suppressing cough while cold air exacerbates it, indicating airway hyperreactivity and thermal sensitivity consistent with cough-variant asthma or laryngeal hypersensitivity requiring bronchial provocation testing.

Doctor_English: How long have you had this cough — days, weeks, or months — and has it been getting better or worse?
Patient_Yoruba: Ikọ́ mi ti wà fún oṣù mẹ́ta. Ó ti ń burú síwájú sí i ní gbogbo ọ̀sẹ̀ tí ó kọjá. Mi ò gbà pé yóò pẹ́ tó bẹ́ẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Chronic productive cough of three months duration with progressive worsening, meeting the definition of chronic cough and mandating investigation for tuberculosis, bronchiectasis, and malignancy regardless of the absence of constitutional symptoms.

Doctor_English: How long have you been experiencing this chest pain — is this the first time, or has it happened before?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora àyà mi ti wà fún ọjọ́ méjì. Mo ti ní irora bẹ́ẹ̀ lẹ́ẹ̀mejì ṣáájú ṣùgbọ́n kò le bíi ìgbà yìí. Ìgbà yìí ni ó le jù.
Clinical_Translation_English: Recurrent chest pain with the current episode being the most severe, indicating a possible acute coronary syndrome superimposed on prior stable angina, requiring immediate ECG, cardiac biomarkers, and risk stratification for unstable angina or NSTEMI.

Doctor_English: How long have you had the swelling in your abdomen — has it been there for years or is it new?
Patient_Yoruba: Ikùn mi ti ń pọ̀ sí i díẹ̀díẹ̀ fún ọdún méjì. Kò wáyé lójijì. Mo rò pé o jẹ́ nítorí oúnjẹ ṣáájú.
Clinical_Translation_English: Slowly progressive abdominal distension over two years attributed to diet by the patient, but the chronicity and insidious onset suggest hepatic cirrhosis with ascites, portal hypertension, or an intra-abdominal malignancy requiring urgent imaging and ascitic tap.

Doctor_English: How long have the headaches been going on, and have you needed to take more painkillers over time to get the same effect?
Patient_Yoruba: Orí mi ti ń fọ̀ fún ọdún kan. Mo ti bẹ̀rẹ̀ pẹ̀lú tábùlẹ́tì méjì ní ọjọ́ kan ṣùgbọ́n báyìí mo nílò mẹ́fà láti gba ìtura kan náà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Escalating analgesic requirement over one year for headache control, fulfilling the diagnostic criteria for medication overuse headache where frequent analgesia perpetuates a cycle of rebound headaches, requiring analgesic withdrawal and preventive therapy.

Doctor_English: How long have you had difficulty falling asleep, and was there anything that happened around the time it started?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìṣòro ìsùn mi bẹ̀rẹ̀ lẹ́yìn tí mo pàdánù iṣẹ́ mi ní oṣù mẹ́fà sẹ́yìn. Láàárọ̀ tí ó tó ìyẹn, mo sùn dáradára.
Clinical_Translation_English: Onset of insomnia temporally linked to job loss six months ago, establishing a psychosocial precipitant consistent with adjustment disorder or major depressive episode, guiding treatment toward cognitive-behavioural therapy for insomnia alongside psychosocial support.

Doctor_English: How long have you had this skin rash, and does it look the same as when it first appeared?
Patient_Yoruba: Àpá náà ti wà fún ọ̀sẹ̀ mẹ́fà. Ó ti yí padà láti irú ìwọ̀ pupa díẹ̀ sí ẹ̀gbọ̀n tó gbẹ tó ń yọ báyìí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Evolving rash over six weeks with morphological change from erythematous macules to hyperkeratotic plaques, indicating a dynamic process such as psoriasis guttate evolving to plaque type, or a mycosis requiring skin biopsy to exclude fungal infection or cutaneous lymphoma.

Doctor_English: How long have you been experiencing this weakness in your hand, and is it affecting your dominant hand?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọwọ́ ọ̀tún mi ti ń di àìlera fún oṣù mẹ́fà. Ó ń ṣe mí ìṣòro láti kọ àti mu nǹkan. Ọwọ́ ọ̀tún ni mo ń lo fún gbogbo nǹkan.
Clinical_Translation_English: Progressive dominant hand weakness over six months causing writing and grip dysfunction, indicating a focal neurological lesion affecting the corticospinal tract or peripheral nerve supply to the hand requiring MRI brain and cervical spine, nerve conduction studies, and EMG.

Doctor_English: How long have you had blood in your urine, and has the amount been increasing?
Patient_Yoruba: Ẹjẹ̀ ti wà nínú ito mi fún oṣù méjì. Ní ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ díẹ̀ ni mo rí i ṣùgbọ́n báyìí ito mi pupa pátápátá ní ìgbà-ìgbà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Progressive haematuria over two months with increasing blood volume, a red flag symptom for urothelial malignancy of the bladder, ureter, or renal pelvis, requiring urgent cystoscopy, upper tract imaging, and urine cytology.

Doctor_English: How long have you had this lump, and has anyone examined it before?
Patient_Yoruba: Èso yìí wà fún oṣù mẹ́fa. Kò sí ẹni tó wo rẹ̀ rárá. Mo rò pé yóò lọ fúnra rẹ̀ ṣùgbọ́n kò lọ.
Clinical_Translation_English: Persistent lump of six months duration with no prior clinical assessment, exceeding the two-week urgent referral threshold for unexplained lumps, requiring immediate physical examination, imaging, and biopsy as appropriate to the anatomical site.

Doctor_English: How long have you been noticing this change in your bowel habit, and is it associated with any weight loss?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìgbẹ́ mi ti yí padà fún oṣù mẹ́fà. Mo ti tẹ̀nìí pẹ̀lú rẹ̀ láìgbìyànjú. Ó ń jẹ mí ìbànújẹ́ gan-an.
Clinical_Translation_English: Six months of altered bowel habit with concurrent unintentional weight loss constituting dual red flag symptoms for colorectal carcinoma, requiring urgent colonoscopy and CT staging regardless of patient age or absence of rectal bleeding.

Doctor_English: How long have you had this low mood, and have there been any periods in between when you felt normal?
Patient_Yoruba: Inú mi ti ń dùn fún oṣù mẹ́rin láìsí ìsinmi. Ko sí ọjọ́ kan tí mo ti nímọ̀lára dáradára nínú ìgbà yẹn.
Clinical_Translation_English: Persistent depressed mood for four months without euthymic intervals, meeting duration criteria for a major depressive episode and requiring PHQ-9 scoring, suicide risk assessment, and antidepressant or psychological therapy initiation.

Doctor_English: How long have you had this persistent dry cough since starting your blood pressure tablet?
Patient_Yoruba: Mo bẹ̀rẹ̀ oògùn ìpàná ẹjẹ̀ tuntun ní oṣù mẹ́fà sẹ́yìn. Ikọ́ gbígbẹ mi bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ọ̀sẹ̀ méjì lẹ́yìn. Ko dára rárá.
Clinical_Translation_English: ACE inhibitor-induced dry cough developing two weeks after initiation and persisting for six months, the most common side effect of this drug class occurring in up to twenty percent of patients, requiring switch to an angiotensin receptor blocker as an alternative.

Doctor_English: How long have you had difficulty swallowing, and at what point did it start affecting liquids as well as solids?
Patient_Yoruba: Gbígbé ohun tó le ti ṣòro fún oṣù méjì. Ní ọ̀sẹ̀ kan sẹ́yìn, omi tún bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ṣòro. Ó ń burú gan-an báyìí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Progressive dysphagia to solids for two months with recent onset dysphagia to liquids, the alarming temporal progression of complete oesophageal obstruction from malignancy or severe stricture, requiring emergency endoscopy to prevent complete obstruction and aspiration.

Doctor_English: How long have you had this ringing in your ear, and does it ever stop completely?
Patient_Yoruba: Etí mi ń rò fún oṣù mẹ́fà. Ko dáwọ̀ rárá, ó wà ní ìgbà gbogbo. Ó ti ń dẹ́kun ìsùn mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Constant unremitting tinnitus for six months causing sleep disruption, indicating chronic rather than episodic pathology, requiring audiological assessment for sensorineural hearing loss, MRI of the internal auditory meati to exclude acoustic neuroma, and tinnitus retraining therapy.

Doctor_English: How long has the child had this fever, and have there been any days when the temperature completely returned to normal?
Patient_Yoruba: Ọmọ mi ní ibà fún ọjọ́ mẹ́wàá. Ní ọjọ́ mẹ́ta, o dára díẹ̀ ṣùgbọ́n ibà padà ní ọjọ́ kẹrin. Ko parí rárá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Fever of ten days duration with incomplete resolution and relapse on day four, indicating an inadequately treated bacterial infection, drug-resistant malaria, typhoid fever, or a non-infectious fever from juvenile idiopathic arthritis or malignancy requiring systematic investigation.

Doctor_English: Can you point to exactly where the pain is worst — does it stay in one spot or move around?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora náà wà ní ibi tó tọ̀ yìí nìkan, ní ẹ̀gbẹ́ ọ̀tún ikùn mi. Ko ń rìn sí ibi mìíràn rárá.
Clinical_Translation_English: Fixed point tenderness in the right iliac fossa without radiation, distinguishing appendicitis from the migratory pain of mesenteric adenitis or the radiating colic of ureteric stone, guiding toward surgical assessment and localised ultrasound imaging.

Doctor_English: Is the numbness confined to the tips of your fingers, the whole hand, or does it go up your arm?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìmọ̀ ọwọ́ mi kò wà ní àwọn ìlà ọwọ́ mi mẹ́ta, tó ń rọ sínu ọwọ́ mi ṣùgbọ́n kò gùn sínu apá mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Numbness confined to the median nerve distribution of the first three fingers and palm without proximal extension, the anatomical distribution of carpal tunnel syndrome, confirmed by Tinel's and Phalen's tests and nerve conduction studies.

Doctor_English: Is the swelling in the middle of your neck, to one side, or is it behind your ear?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìwọ̀n náà wà ní ẹ̀gbẹ́ ọ̀tún ọrùn mi, ní ìsàlẹ̀ ẹrẹ̀kẹ́. Ko wà ní àárín tàbí ẹ̀yìn etí.
Clinical_Translation_English: Lateral neck lump below the mandible in the anterior triangle, a location consistent with lymphadenopathy, branchial cyst, or parotid tail tumour, requiring ultrasound and fine needle aspiration cytology to characterise the lesion.

Doctor_English: Is the pain specifically over the shin bone, or is it in the calf muscle behind the leg?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora wà ní iwájú ẹsẹ̀ mi, lórí eegun funrararẹ̀. Kò wà ní ẹ̀hin ẹsẹ̀. Ó ń dùn nígbà tí mo bá tẹ eegun náà.
Clinical_Translation_English: Anterior shin bone tenderness on direct palpation distinguishing tibial stress fracture from the calf muscle pain of deep vein thrombosis or gastrocnemius strain, requiring plain X-ray and MRI for suspected stress fracture in an athletic patient.

Doctor_English: Is the rash on your palms and soles as well as on the rest of your body?
Patient_Yoruba: Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, àpá náà wà lórí àtẹ̀lẹ ọwọ́ mi àti àtẹ̀lẹ ẹsẹ̀ mi pẹ̀lú. Kì í ṣe nínú ẹnu mi.
Clinical_Translation_English: Palmoplantar involvement of a rash is a distinctive distribution occurring in secondary syphilis, reactive arthritis, hand-foot-and-mouth disease, and erythema multiforme, each requiring distinct investigation and management.

Doctor_English: Is the weakness affecting the muscles closer to your body, like your hips and shoulders, or the ones further away like your hands and feet?
Patient_Yoruba: Ìbàdí mi àti èjìká mi ni ó ń ṣe mí ìṣòro jù. Mo ń rí ìṣòro láti dìde àga àti gbé ọwọ́ mi ga. Ọwọ́ mi fúnrararẹ̀ ṣiṣẹ́ dáradára.
Clinical_Translation_English: Proximal myopathy with preserved distal function, the pattern of inflammatory myopathy such as polymyositis, dermatomyositis, or metabolic myopathy from hypothyroidism or statin use, distinguished from distal weakness of peripheral neuropathy.

Doctor_English: Is the abdominal tenderness in the upper middle, right upper, or lower right area of the abdomen?
Patient_Yoruba: Nígbà tí dọ́kítà bá tẹ ìsàlẹ̀ àyà mi ní àárín, irora ń wáyé lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀. Ó kò dùn ní ẹ̀gbẹ́ tàbí ìsàlẹ̀.
Clinical_Translation_English: Epigastric tenderness on deep palpation without lateral or lower quadrant involvement, consistent with gastric or duodenal pathology, pancreatitis, or hepatitis, guiding toward upper endoscopy, amylase, lipase, and liver function testing.

Doctor_English: Is the joint pain in the small joints of your fingers, large joints like your knees, or both?
Patient_Yoruba: Àwọn ìpín kéékèéké ní ọwọ́ mi méjèéjì ni ó ń dùn jù. Ó pàápàá buru nígbà owúrọ̀. Orúkún mi ń dùn díẹ̀ pẹ̀lú.
Clinical_Translation_English: Symmetrical small joint polyarthritis of the hands with morning stiffness and secondary large joint involvement, the classic distribution of rheumatoid arthritis requiring rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP antibody, and X-ray of the hands and feet.

Doctor_English: Is the pain in your lower back, in the buttock, or does it start in one place and shoot to the other?
Patient_Yoruba: Irora bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ẹ̀hin ìsàlẹ̀ mi, lẹ́yìn náà ó ń rọ sínú ìdí mi ọ̀tún àti sísàlẹ̀ ẹsẹ̀ mi.
